When he brought that in which there was no room for doubt, he found no way out but to slander him as being a "skilled magician (ساحر عليم)."

Therefore, he followed his accusation of magic with His saying: "He desires to expel you from your land by his magic," to incite them against him and urge them to agree with him on repelling him by any possible means.

His saying: فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ "So what do you advise?" Perhaps what is meant by "commanding (الأمر)" is suggesting to him, since the suggester suggests to the one who consults him using the wording of a command. So the meaning is: "If the matter is such, then what do you suggest I treat him with so that I may act upon it?" This is because he viewed himself as their highest lord and viewed them as his slaves, and it would not be appropriate to take "command (الأمر)" in its conventional sense.

This meaning is supported by the fact that He, the Exalted, recounted this speech in another place from the elite themselves, as He said: قَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَسَاحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ * يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُمْ ۖ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ "The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, 'This is indeed a skilled magician. He desires to expel you from your land. So what do you advise?'" (7:110-111). It is apparent that what is meant by their "commanding" is their suggesting to Pharaoh that he do such-and-such to them.

It is said: The authority of the miracle overwhelmed and dazed him, so he strayed from his wonder and arrogance, and wretchedness overcame him, so he did not know what to say or how to speak.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَابْعَثْ فِي الْمَدَائِنِ حَاشِرِينَ * يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ "They said, 'Put him and his brother off [for a while], and send heralds to the cities, to bring you every skilled magician'" (26:36-37). The speakers are the elite around him, who were the nobles of his people. His saying: أَرْجِهْ (Put him off) with the sukun on the ha according to the common recitation is a command from الإرجاء, meaning delay. That is: delay Musa and his brother and give them respite, and do not hasten to punish or imprison them or the like, until you oppose their magic with magic like it.

It is also recited as أَرْجِهِ with the kasra on the ha and أرجئه with the hamza and the damma on the ha; both are more eloquent than the common recitation, and the meaning is the same in any case.

His saying: وَابْعَثْ فِي الْمَدَائِنِ حَاشِرِينَ "and send heralds to the cities." المدائن is the plural of مدينة, which is the town. الحاشر (the herald/gatherer) is from الحشر, which is to bring out to a place with agitation. That is: send into the lands a number of your officers and soldiers to gather every skilled magician within them and bring them to you so that you may oppose the two of them with their magic.

The use of the intensive form سَحَّار (very skilled magician) instead of ساحر (magician) is to point out that there are those who are more knowledgeable than him in the arts of magic and more prolific in practice.

His saying, the Exalted: فَجُمِعَ السَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقَاتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ "So the magicians were gathered for the tryst of an appointed day" (26:38). It is the Day of Adornment (يوم الزينة) which Musa and Pharaoh agreed to make the appointed time for the confrontation, as in Surah Ta-Ha. Thus, there is conciseness and summary in the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ * لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ السَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا هُمُ الْغَالِبِينَ "And the people were asked, 'Will you all gather? Maybe we will follow the magicians if they are the victors'" (26:39-40). The interrogation is to urge and encourage the people to gather.

It is stated in al-Kashshaf that the summary of the meaning is: what is meant by "following the magicians" is following them in their religion—and they were ostensibly worshippers of Pharaoh, as appears from the context of the following verses. Their intention by this was only that they should not follow Musa, not the following of the magicians. They only cast their speech in the form of a metaphor to incite the magicians to concern and seriousness in the struggle for victory.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جَاءَ السَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغَالِبِينَ * قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ "So when the magicians came, they said to Pharaoh, 'Shall we have a reward if we are the victors?' He said, 'Yes, and you will then be among those who are near [to me]'" (26:41-42). The interrogation carries the meaning of a request. They said: "if we are (إن كنا)" and did not say "when we are (إذا كنا)" the victors, to avoid expressing certainty of victory—as is expressed by their later saying: "By the might of Pharaoh, we shall indeed be the victors"—but rather they cast it in the form of doubt so that it would be more inviting for Pharaoh to grant the reward.

This had its effect, as he granted them a reward and added to it the promise of making them among those near to him.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰ أَلْقُوا — up to His saying — تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ "Moses said to them, 'Throw down...' [and behold, it] swallowed what they had faked" (26:43-45). الحبال is the plural of حبل (rope), and العصي is the plural of عصا (staff). اللقف is to swallow quickly. ما يأفكون is from الإفك, meaning to turn a thing away from its proper face; magic was called إفك because it involves turning a thing from its real form to an imaginary form. The meaning of the verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ ساجِدِينَ قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ رَبِّ مُوسى وَهارُونَ — "Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds, the Lord of Moses and Aaron.’" (26:46-48). This means that when the magicians witnessed the dazzling signs (الآيات الباهرة) they saw, they were so overwhelmed and astonished that they could not restrain themselves from falling prostrate upon the earth before Allah, the Glorified. The term "thrown" (الإلقاء) is used metaphorically for their falling to the ground to indicate their lack of self-control, as if they had been cast down upon the earth.

His saying: قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds’" (26:47) contains an expression of faith in Allah, the Glorified—a faith of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. This is because, as previously mentioned, acknowledging Him, the Exalted, as the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين) cannot be complete except with monotheism and the negation of any deities besides Him.

His saying: رَبِّ مُوسى وَهارُونَ — "the Lord of Moses and Aaron" (26:48) contains an allusion to belief in the message (الرسالة) in addition to monotheism.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "He said, ‘Did you believe in him before I should give you leave? He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic. Soon you will know!’" (26:49) until the end of the verse. The speaker is Pharaoh. The meaning of his saying: آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ — "Did you believe in him before I should give you leave?" is: "You believed without permission from me," similar to the usage in His saying, the Exalted: لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِماتُ رَبِّي — "the sea would surely be exhausted before the words of my Lord are exhausted" (18:109). It does not imply that permission was possible or expected from him, as some have suggested.

His saying: إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ — "He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic" is another slander (بهتان) he fabricated against Moses (عليه السلام) to turn the hearts of his people, and specifically their elite (الملأ), away from him.

His saying: فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — "Soon you will know!" is a threat against them delivered in an ambiguous (الإبهام) context to indicate that he has no need to specify the punishment, for they shall soon experience it.

His saying: لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, and I will surely crucify you all" (26:49). Cutting "on opposite sides" (من خلاف) means cutting the right hand with the left foot, or vice versa. Crucifixion (التصليب) is to place the criminal upon a cross. A similar verse has already been discussed in Surah al-A'raf and Surah Ta-Ha.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا لا ضَيْرَ إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ — "They said, ‘[There is] no harm [in that]! Indeed we shall return to our Lord.’" (26:50). The word الضير means harm (الضرر). Their saying: إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ — "Indeed we shall return to our Lord" is the justification (تعليل) for their statement "no harm"; meaning: "We shall not be harmed by this punishment with which you threaten us, for we shall remain patient and thereby return to our Lord—and what a noble return that is!"

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ لَنا رَبُّنا خَطايانا أَنْ كُنَّا أَوَّلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Indeed we hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins for our being the first of the believers." (26:51). This is the justification for what is understood from their previous speech—that they do not fear death or being killed, but rather long for the meeting with their Lord. They are saying: "We do not fear anything of your punishment because we return through it to our Lord, and we do not fear the return because we hope our Lord will forgive us our sins due to our being the first to believe in Moses and Aaron, the two messengers of our Lord."

Opening the door to any good (خير) has an effect of goodness that the sound intellect (العقل السليم) does not doubt. Thus, if Allah, the Glorified, were to honor a believer with forgiveness and mercy for his faith, His forgiveness and mercy would not bypass the very first ones to open this door and enter this path.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ — "We revealed to Moses, [saying], ‘Set out with My servants by night, [for] you will be pursued.’" (26:52). This is the beginning of the narration of the second part of the story, which is the description of the punishment of the House of Pharaoh due to their rejection of the call of Moses and Aaron (عليه السلام). The first part was the message of Moses and Aaron to them and their call to monotheism. The terms الإسراء and السري refer to traveling by night. The meaning of "My servants" (عبادي) is the Children of Israel, and in this expression, there is a form of honoring them.

His saying: إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ — "you will be pursued" is the justification for the command; meaning: "Travel with them by night so that the House of Pharaoh may pursue you." In this, there is an indication that Allah had a purpose in their pursuit and that therein lay the deliverance of the Children of Israel. He stated this explicitly in His saying: فَأَسْرِ بِعِبادِي لَيْلاً إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ وَاتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً إِنَّهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُغْرَقُونَ — "‘Set out with My servants by night, [for] you will be pursued. And leave the sea parted: they are a host that is to be drowned.’" (44:23-24).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي الْمَدائِنِ حاشِرِينَ — "Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities" (26:53) until His saying: ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ — "Then We drowned the rest" (26:66). This is the story of the drowning of the House of Pharaoh and the salvation of the Children of Israel in fourteen verses. The discourse is concise, omitting some stages of the story because they are evident from the context, such as the departure of Moses and the Children of Israel by night from Egypt, as indicated by His saying: أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي — "Set out with My servants by night."

Thus, He, the Exalted, said: فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ — "Then Pharaoh sent"; meaning: Moses traveled by night with My servants, and when Pharaoh learned of this, he sent men into the cities (المدائن) under his authority as "gatherers" (حاشرِينَ) to muster the people and assemble the crowds, saying to the people: إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ — "Indeed these"—referring to the Children of Israel— لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ — "are a small band" (26:54). The term الشرذمة refers to the small remainder of anything, so describing them as "small" (قليلون) is an emphasis. وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنا لَغائِظُونَ — "and they have indeed enraged us" (26:55) by performing deeds that provoke our anger. وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ — "and we are a host" united in what we resolve upon, حاذِرُونَ — "on our guard" (26:56), wary of the enemy lest they assassinate us or plot against us, even if they are weak and few. The objective of these statements—which were undoubtedly a proclamation from Pharaoh—was to incite the people against them.

فَأَخْرَجْناهُمْ مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَكُنُوزٍ وَمَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ — "So We expelled them from gardens and springs, and treasures and a noble station" (26:57-58), which included their lofty palaces and high houses. Since their departure was due to a divine plan (مكر إلهي) triggered by the impulse of superiority and arrogance (الاستكبار) within them, He attributed their expulsion to Himself. كَذلِكَ — "So it was" (26:59); meaning the matter was thus. وَأَوْرَثْناها — "and We made them an inheritance for" —meaning those gardens, springs, treasures, and the noble station— بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ — "the Children of Israel," as We destroyed Pharaoh and his hosts and kept the Children of Israel after them, so they became the inheritors.

فَأَتْبَعُوهُمْ — "So they pursued them" —meaning they caught up with the Children of Israel— مُشْرِقِينَ — "at sunrise" (26:60); meaning entering the time of the sun's rising. فَلَمَّا تَراءَا الْجَمْعانِ — "When the two hosts sighted each other" (26:61); meaning they drew near to one another such that each of the two groups—the host of Pharaoh and the host of Moses—saw the other. قالَ أَصْحابُ مُوسى — "the companions of Moses said," from among the Children of Israel, in fear and panic: إِنَّ لَمُدْرَكُونَ — "‘We are indeed overtaken!’" Pharaoh's soldiers will surely catch us.

قالَ — "He said," Moses: كَلَّا — "‘No!’" They shall never overtake us. إِنَّ مَعِي رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ — "‘Indeed my Lord is with me. He will guide me.’" (26:62). What is meant by this "withness" (المعية) is the withness of protection and support, which is what his Lord promised him when He first sent him and his brother to Pharaoh: إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما — "Indeed I am with you both" (20:46). As for the withness of origination and governance (التدبير), Allah, the Glorified, is with Moses and Pharaoh in equal measure. His saying: سَيَهْدِينِ — "He will guide me" means: He will direct me to a path by which Pharaoh cannot reach me.

فأَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى أَنِ اضْرِبْ بِعَصاكَ الْبَحْرَ فَانْفَلَقَ — "Then We revealed to Moses, [saying], ‘Strike the sea with your staff.’ Thereupon it parted" (26:63). Parting (الانفلاق) is the splitting of a thing and the separation of its parts from one another. فَكانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ — "and each part" —meaning a separated piece of water— كَالطَّوْدِ — "was like a mountain" الْعَظِيمِ — "massive." So Moses and those with him from the Children of Israel entered it.

وَأَزْلَفْنا ثَمَ — "And there We brought near" الْآخَرِينَ — "the others" (26:64), who were Pharaoh and his hosts. وَأَنْجَيْنا مُوسى وَمَنْ مَعَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ — "And We delivered Moses and all those who were with him" (26:65) by maintaining the sea in its state and form until they crossed and exited it. ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ — "Then We drowned the rest" (26:66) by closing the sea upon them while they were within its parting.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ — "There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (26:67-68). The apparent context—supported by the context of the stories that follow—is that the referent is the sum total of what was mentioned in the story of Moses: his being sent, his calling Pharaoh and his people, the deliverance of the Children of Israel, and the drowning of Pharaoh and his hosts. In all of that is a sign (آية) indicating His monotheism in Lordship (الربوبية) and the truth of the message for whoever reflects upon it.

His saying: وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "but most of them do not have faith" means that most of those whose story We have mentioned were not believers despite the clarity of the sign indicated. Accordingly, His saying after each of the stories cited in the Surah: وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ is like drawing a conclusion and applying the example to the one for whom the example is set. It is as if it is said after citing each story: "This is their story containing His sign, yet most of them were not believers, just as most of your people do not believe; so do not grieve over them, for this is the habit of all the nations to whom We sent a messenger who called them to the monotheism of Lordship."

It has been said that the pronoun in "most of them" (أكثرهم) refers to the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the meaning is: "In this story is a sign, yet most of your people do not believe in it." However, this is not without some far-fetchedness.

His saying: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ — "Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful" has already been explained at the beginning of the Surah.

Ayahs

وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٦٩)
Relate to them the account of Abraham (69)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٧٠)
when he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you are worshiping?!’ (70)
قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَـٰكِفِينَ (٧١)
They said, ‘We worship idols, and are constant in our devotion to them.’ (71)
قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ (٧٢)
He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call them? (72)
أَوْ يَنفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ (٧٣)
Or do they bring you any benefit, or cause you any harm?’ (73)
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا كَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٤)
They said, ‘Indeed, we found our fathers doing likewise.’ (74)
قَالَ أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ (٧٥)
He said, ‘Have you regarded what you have been worshipping, (75)
أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمُ ٱلْأَقْدَمُونَ (٧٦)
you and your ancestors? (76)
فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِّىٓ إِلَّا رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٧)
They are indeed enemies to me, but the Lord of all the worlds, (77)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَنِى فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ (٧٨)
who created me, it is He who guides me, (78)
وَٱلَّذِى هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِى وَيَسْقِينِ (٧٩)
and provides me with food and drink, (79)
وَإِذَا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ (٨٠)
and when I get sick, it is He who cures me; (80)
وَٱلَّذِى يُمِيتُنِى ثُمَّ يُحْيِينِ (٨١)
who will make me die, then He will bring me to life, (81)
وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لِى خَطِيٓـَٔتِى يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (٨٢)
and who, I hope, will forgive me my faults on the Day of Retribution.’ (82)
رَبِّ هَبْ لِى حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٣)
‘My Lord! Grant me [unerring] judgement, and unite me with the Righteous. (83)
وَٱجْعَل لِّى لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٨٤)
Confer on me a worthy repute among the posterity, (84)
وَٱجْعَلْنِى مِن وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٨٥)
and make me one of the heirs to the paradise of bliss. (85)
وَٱغْفِرْ لِأَبِىٓ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٨٦)
Forgive my father, for he is one of those who are astray. (86)
وَلَا تُخْزِنِى يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٨٧)
Do not disgrace me on the day that they will be resurrected, (87)
يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ (٨٨)
the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, (88)
إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى ٱللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (٨٩)
except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart,’ (89)
وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (٩٠)
and paradise will be brought near for the Godwary, (90)
وَبُرِّزَتِ ٱلْجَحِيمُ لِلْغَاوِينَ (٩١)
and hell will be brought into view for the perverse, (91)
وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ (٩٢)
and they shall be told: ‘Where is that which you used to worship (92)
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ هَلْ يَنصُرُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَنتَصِرُونَ (٩٣)
besides Allah? Do they help you, or do they help each other?’ (93)
فَكُبْكِبُوا۟ فِيهَا هُمْ وَٱلْغَاوُۥنَ (٩٤)
Then they will be cast into it on their faces—they and the perverse, (94)
وَجُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ أَجْمَعُونَ (٩٥)
and the hosts of Iblis all together. (95)
قَالُوا۟ وَهُمْ فِيهَا يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٩٦)
They will say, as they wrangle in it [together], (96)
تَٱللَّهِ إِن كُنَّا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٩٧)
‘By Allah, we had indeed been in manifest error (97)
إِذْ نُسَوِّيكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٨)
when we equated you with the Lord of all the worlds! (98)
وَمَآ أَضَلَّنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٩٩)
No one led us astray except the guilty. (99)
فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَـٰفِعِينَ (١٠٠)
Now we have no intercessors, (100)
وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ (١٠١)
nor do we have any sympathetic friend. (101)
فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٢)
Had there been another turn for us, we would be among the faithful.’ (102)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٠٣)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (103)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٠٤)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (104)

(Commentary)

After concluding the story of Moses, the verses point toward the report of Abraham (عليه‌السلام). This is his momentous account when he rose to establish monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — by his pure and pristine primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — from among his people who were united in the worship of idols. He disassociated himself from them and defended the Truth, and then his affair unfolded as it did. In that, there is a sign (آية), yet most of his people did not believe, as will be indicated at the end of the verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ إِبْراهِيمَ — "Relate to them the account of Abraham" (26:69). The context changes from how it was at the beginning of the story of Moses: وَإِذْ نادى رَبُّكَ مُوسى — "When your Lord called out to Moses..." (26:10), because of His saying: عَلَيْهِمْ — "to them." The objective is to recite it to the polytheists of the Arabs, primarily the Quraysh. This Abraham is their forefather, and he rose to spread monotheism and establish the true religion (الدين) — the divine system of beliefs and laws — when there was no one among them at that time saying, "There is no god but Allah." Thus, Allah aided him and he was victorious until the word of monotheism was firmly established in the Holy Land and in the Hijaz.

All of this occurred only through a call from the primordial nature (الفطرة) and a commission from Allah, the Glorified. In this, there is a sign (آية) of Allah; so let them take heed and disassociate themselves from the religion of idolatry, just as their father Abraham (عليه‌السلام) disassociated himself from it and from his father and his people who were practitioners of it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ ما تَعْبُدُونَ — "When he said to his father and his people, 'What do you worship?'" (26:70). His (عليه‌السلام) dispute and debate with his father was distinct from his dispute with his people and his argumentation against them, as narrated by Allah the Exalted in Surah al-An'am and elsewhere. However, the structure here is based on brevity and conciseness; therefore, He combined the two arguments and cast them as a single debate, mentioning what is common between them.

His saying: ما تَعْبُدُونَ — "What do you worship?" is a question about reality (الحقيقة), placing himself in the position of one who knows nothing of its essence or its other affairs. This is one of the methods of debate (المناظرة) — the path of one who wishes for the opponent to clarify the reality of his claim and its details so that he may hold him accountable by his own admission.

Furthermore, this debate took place when Abraham first emerged from his cave and entered the society of his father and his people, having witnessed none of this before that day. Thus, he argued against them from a simple, pure primordial nature (الفطرة), as detailed previously in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah al-An'am.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا نَعْبُدُ أَصْناماً فَنَظَلُّ لَها عاكِفِينَ — "They said, 'We worship idols, and we are constant in our devotion to them'" (26:71). The word ظل means "to continue" or "to persist," and العكوف (devotion) toward something is to remain with it and stay by it. The preposition ل (for/to) in لَها is for causation; meaning, we continue to be devoted to them because of them, which is a branch of the worship of idols.

The idol (الصنم) is a physical body made of metal, wood, or other materials in a specific form, intended to represent the attributes of the deity. These people used to worship angels and jinn, believing them to be spiritual beings (روحانيات) outside the world of bodies, transcendent (منزهة) from the properties and effects of matter. Since it was difficult for them to direct their worshipful attention toward these spiritual beings by summoning them to their perception, they sought a means to do so by adopting physical forms and statues that represented those spiritual meanings through their shapes and figures.

The same applies to the worshipers of the stars; the original deity there was the spiritualities of the stars. They then took the celestial bodies of the stars as idols for those spiritualities. Later, when the states of the stars varied through presence, absence, rising, and setting, they adopted idols for them that represented the active powers (القوى الفعالة) the stars possessed over the world of elements below them—such as the power causing joy, pleasure, and vigor in Venus, which they depicted as a young woman; or for bloodshed in Mars; or for knowledge and gnosis in Mercury. The same logic applies to the idols of saintly humans.

Thus, idols were only adopted so that each would be a mirror for the "Lord of the Idol" (رب الصنم)—whether an angel, jinn, or human. However, they ended up worshipping the idol itself by directing worship toward it and seeking proximity to it. Even if they bypassed the idol to its "Lord," they would still be worshipping it instead of Allah, the Glorified.

This is what belies the claim of those among them who say: "The idol is merely a direction of prayer (قبلة), adopted only as a point for worshipful orientation, not intended for its own sake, like the Kaaba for Muslims." This is because a qibla is that which one faces during worship, not that which one faces with worship. They face the idol in worship and with worship. In other words, the orientation toward the qibla is for the sake of the Lord of the qibla, who is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه); whereas with the idol, the orientation is toward it and the worship is for it, not for its "Lord." Even if we assume the worship is for its "Lord"—which is some spiritual being—it would be for that being, not for Allah. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is not the one worshipped in that case under any circumstances.

In short, their answer to Abraham's question: ما تَعْبُدُونَ ("What do you worship?") with their statement: نَعْبُدُ أَصْناماً ("We worship idols") is a clarification that these worshipped bodies are representations intended for something else, not for themselves. Abraham took their word نَعْبُدُ ("We worship") and disputed with them using it; for the independence of idols in being worshipped is incompatible with them being mere representations of another. Since they were the objects of worship, they must necessarily possess that which is the intended purpose of worship—such as bringing benefit or averting harm through worshipful orientation, supplication (الدعاء), and petitioning. Yet idols are far removed from knowing a petition or answering a person in distress by delivering benefit or diverting harm. Therefore, Abraham asked them: هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ ("Do they hear you?"), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ أَوْ يَنْفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ — "He said, 'Do they hear you when you call [them]? Or do they benefit you, or harm?'" (26:72-73). He (عليه‌السلام) challenged them regarding their worship of idols from two perspectives:

First: Worship is a representation of the worshiper's humility and need for the worshipped; thus, it is never devoid of a supplication (دعاء) from the worshiper to the worshipped. Supplication depends on the worshipped having knowledge of it and hearing what is being asked, yet idols are inanimate bodies (أجسام جمادية) that have no hearing; thus, there is no meaning in worshipping them.

Second: People only worship a deity either out of desire for its goodness and benefit or out of protection from its evil and harm. Idols are inanimate objects with no power to deliver benefit or avert harm.

Each of the two verses contains one of these two aspects of the challenge, and he presented them in the form of a question to compel them to confess.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنا آباءَنا كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ — "They said, 'Rather we found our fathers acting thus'" (26:74). The context required them to answer his (عليه‌السلام) question with a negative, but since that would lead to a conclusion contrary to their adherence to idolatry, they turned away from it to cling to the hem of blind imitation (التقليد). They mentioned that they had no basis for worshipping them except pure imitation of their forefathers.

His saying: وَجَدْنا آباءَنا كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ means: "So we did as they used to do and worshipped them as they used to worship." He did not deviate from saying كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ ("acting thus") to something like "worshipping them" so that it would be more explicit in expressing blind imitation—as if they understood nothing of these acts of worship except that they were actions like the actions of their fathers, without comprehending anything beyond their forms and images.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمُ الْأَقْدَمُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِي إِلَّا رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ — "He said, 'Have you considered what you have been worshipping, you and your ancient fathers? They are indeed enemies to me, except the Lord of all the worlds'" (26:75-77). When his debate with his father and his people concluded with the fact that they had no proof for their worship of idols except pure imitation of their forefathers, he (عليه‌السلام) disassociated himself from their gods, from themselves, and from their fathers by saying: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ("Have you considered?"), etc.

His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمُ الْأَقْدَمُونَ is a branch of what became clear from the preceding—the lack of evidence for worshipping idols, or rather, its fundamental invalidity. That is: "If they are invalid and you have no proof for them except the imitation of your fathers, then these idols that you have seen—meaning these very ones that you and your ancient fathers worship—they are indeed enemies to me, because worshipping them is harmful to my religion and destructive to my soul; thus, they are nothing but an enemy to me."

He mentioned their "ancient fathers" to indicate that he does not adopt blind imitation as they did, and that the antiquity of the era holds no weight for him (عليه‌السلام). Temporal precedence has no effect in nullifying a truth or validating a falsehood. The use of pronouns for rational beings (أولي العقل) in reference to the idols is due to the attribution of worship to them, which necessitates consciousness and intellect; this occurs frequently in the Quran.

His saying: إِلَّا رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ — "except the Lord of all the worlds" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع) from his saying: فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِي; meaning: "But the Lord of all the worlds is not like that."

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ — "He who created me, and it is He who guides me" (26:78) until the end of the description of the Lord of the worlds. When he excepted the Lord of the worlds, exalted is His Name, he described Him with attributes that complete the proof that He, the Exalted, is not an enemy to him, but rather a Merciful Lord who cares for his state, bestows every good upon him, and averts every evil from him. So he said: الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي ("He who created me"), etc. As for the claim of some that his saying الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي is a new sentence (استيناف), it is not to be considered.

His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ — he began with creation (الخلق) because the objective was to demonstrate that the management of his affairs (تدبير أمره) is attributed to Him, the Exalted, by way of providing a judgment based on evidence. The proof that management (التدبير) belongs to Him, the Exalted, is that creation and origination (الإيجاد) belong to Him; for it is clear that creation and management are inseparable in these physical beings of gradual existence (التدريجية الوجود) that complete their existence gradually. It is not rational for creation to belong to one thing and management to another. Since creation and origination belong to Allah, the Glorified, then management belongs to Him as well.

For this reason, he coupled guidance (الهداية) with creation using the "fa" of consequence (فاء التفريع), indicating that He, the Exalted, is the Guide because He is the Creator.

The apparent meaning of his saying: فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ — which is absolute — is that it refers to absolute guidance toward benefits, whether worldly or otherworldly. The use of the present tense (المضارع) signifies continuity (الاستمرار). Thus, the meaning is: "He is the one who created me and continues to guide me to that which contains the felicity of my life since He created me, and He shall never cease to do so." Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to what Allah narrated from Moses when he said to Pharaoh: رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى — "Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50); meaning, He guided it to its benefits, which is general guidance (الهداية العامة).

This is what was pointed to at the beginning of the Surah by His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً — "Have they not regarded the earth, how many of every splendid kind [of plant] We have caused to grow in it? There is indeed a sign in that" (26:7-8), and the establishment of the proof therein has already passed.

Accordingly, the attributes mentioned later in His saying: وَالَّذِي هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِي ("and who feeds me"), etc., are of the category of mentioning the specific after the general; for they are all instances of general guidance—some being guidance toward worldly benefits and others being guidance toward what pertains to the Hereafter.

If the intended meaning of guidance were the specific religious guidance (الهداية الخاصة الدينية), then the listed attributes would follow their own course, and the mention of guidance after creation and its precedence over other blessings and gifts would be because it is the most excellent of blessings after existence itself.

His saying: وَالَّذِي هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِي وَيَسْقِينِ وَإِذا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ — "and who feeds me and waters me, and when I get sick, it is He who cures me" (26:79-80) is like a metonymy (كناية) for the totality of material blessings that Allah provides him to complete deficiencies and fulfill worldly needs. He specifically mentioned the most important of them: feeding, watering, and healing when ill.

From here it becomes clear that his saying: وَإِذا مَرِضْتُ ("and when I get sick") is a preamble and preparation for mentioning healing. The speech is effectively: "He feeds me, waters me, and heals me." For this reason, he attributed sickness to himself so as not to disrupt the intended meaning by mentioning the negation of a blessing amidst the blessings. As for the claim that he attributed sickness to himself despite it being from Allah out of etiquette (التأدب), it is not quite so.

He repeated the relative pronoun (الموصول), saying: الَّذِي هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِي, and did not simply conjunct the attributes to what was in His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ, to indicate that each of the mentioned attributes independently serves to establish that He, the Exalted, is the Lord who manages his affairs, sustains his soul, and answers his call.

His saying: وَالَّذِي يُمِيتُنِي ثُمَّ يُحْيِينِ — "and who makes me die, then He will bring me to life" (26:81) refers to the death decreed for every soul, indicated by His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ — "Every soul shall taste death" (21:35). It is not annihilation or non-existence, but a transition from one abode to another as part of the ongoing general management. The "bringing to life" (الإحياء) refers to the bestowing of life after death.

His saying: وَالَّذِي أَطْمَعُ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ لِي خَطِيئَتِي يَوْمَ الدِّينِ — "and who, I hope, will forgive me my faults on the Day of Retribution" (26:82); meaning the Day of Recompense, which is the Day of Resurrection. He did not speak with certainty regarding forgiveness as he did with the matters mentioned before it, because forgiveness is not by right (الاستحقاق) but is a grace (فضل) from Allah. No one has a right over Allah, the Glorified, for anything. However, He, the Glorified, has decreed for Himself guidance, sustenance, death, and life for every living soul, but He did not decree forgiveness for every sinner. He said: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ — "By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth" (51:23); and He said: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ — "Every soul shall taste death" (21:35); and He said: إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا — "To Him is the return of you all—a true promise of Allah" (10:4); while He said regarding forgiveness: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — "Indeed Allah does not forgive admitting any partners with Him, but He forgives [sins] other than that to whomever He wishes" (4:48).

The attribution of "fault" (الخطيئة) to himself, while he (عليه‌السلام) is a prophet infallible (معصوم) from sin (المعصية), is evidence that the intended meaning of "fault" is other than sin in the sense of disobeying a master's command. Fault and sin (الذنب) have levels that are measured according to the servant's state in his servitude (العبودية), as it is said: "The good deeds of the righteous are the misdeeds of the near-stationed." Indeed, He, the Exalted, said to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنْبِكَ — "and plead for forgiveness for your sin" (40:55).

Thus, a "fault" from the likes of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) is his preoccupation from the pure remembrance of Allah by the necessities of life, such as sleep, eating, drinking, and the like—even if, from another perspective, these are acts of obedience from him (عليه‌السلام). How could it be otherwise when He, the Exalted, explicitly stated that he (عليه‌السلام) was sincerely devoted to Allah, with nothing else sharing Him therein, when He said: إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ — "Indeed We purified them with an exclusive quality: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode" (38:46). We have previously provided a discussion related to this station at the end of the sixth volume and in the stories of Abraham in the seventh volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ — "My Lord! Grant me [right] judgment, and unite me with the righteous" (26:83). When he (عليه‌السلام) mentioned the continuous, successive, and accumulated blessings of his Lord upon him from his creation until the infinite span of subsistence, and thereby depicted the encompassment of divine grace and tenderness, he was seized by the attraction of mercy joined with the poverty of servitude. This called him to manifest his need and spread his petition; so he turned from the third person to direct address and asked what he asked.

His saying: رَبِّ ("My Lord") — he attributed the Lordship to himself after having described Him as the Lord of all the worlds, to stir divine mercy and provoke lordly care for the answering of his prayer and petition.

His saying: هَبْ لِي حُكْماً ("Grant me judgment") — he intends by "judgment" (الحكم) what was mentioned previously in the saying of Moses (عليه‌السلام): فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْماً — "so my Lord granted me judgment" (26:21). It is—as mentioned before—the correctness of vision and opinion in the universal doctrinal and practical sciences, and the application of action upon them, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ — "We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that 'There is no god except Me; so worship Me'" (21:25). This is the revelation of doctrinal and practical sciences gathered by monotheism (التوحيد) and godwariness (التقوى). And His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ — "and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers" (21:73). This is the revelation of guidance and direction toward righteousness in the station of action. The indefiniteness of "judgment" (حكماً) is to magnify its status.

His saying: وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ ("and unite me with the righteous") — righteousness (الصلاح), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the opposite of corruption (الفساد), which is the alteration of a thing from the requirement of its original nature. Thus, its righteousness is its being according to the requirement of its original nature, such that the good and benefit that ought to result from it do so without it being corrupted and thus deprived of its beautiful effects.

Since بِالصَّالِحِينَ ("with the righteous") is not restricted by "action" or the like, the intended meaning is those who are righteous in essence (ذاتاً), not merely in action—even though essential righteousness is inseparable from righteous action. He, the Exalted, said: الْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord" (7:58).

Thus, essential righteousness is the soul being perfectly prepared to receive divine mercy and the effusion of every good and felicity it is meant to be clothed with, without being accompanied by what would corrupt it of false belief or evil deed. By this, it becomes clear that essential righteousness is among the concomitants of the gift of "judgment" in the sense previously mentioned, even though "judgment" is more specific in its application than righteousness, as is apparent.

Therefore, his petition to be united with the righteous is among the concomitants of the petition for the gift of judgment and its branches that follow from it. Thus, the meaning of his saying: رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ returns to something like: "My Lord, grant me judgment and complete its effect in me, which is essential righteousness."

A discussion related to this station has already passed in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "and in the Hereafter he will surely be among the righteous" (2:130) in the first volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and appoint for me a worthy mention among the later generations" (26:84). The attribution of "tongue" (اللسان) to "truth" (الصدق) is possessive, implying its exclusivity to truth such that it speaks nothing but it. The apparent meaning of "appointing this tongue for him" is that it be specific to him like his own tongue, speaking only what is in his heart of what he himself speaks. Thus, the meaning results in a petition that Allah raise among the later generations one who will rise with his call and invite people to his creed (ملته), which is the religion of monotheism.

Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying in Surah al-Saffat after mentioning Abraham (عليه‌السلام): وَتَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ — "and We left for him [a good name] among the later generations" (37:108). He mentioned this sentence after mentioning several other prophets besides him, such as Noah, Moses, Aaron, and Elias. Similarly, He, the Exalted, said in Surah Maryam after mentioning Zechariah, John, Jesus, Abraham, Moses, and Aaron: وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ لِسانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا — "and We appointed for them a worthy and lofty mention" (19:50). The intended meaning in any case is the preservation of their call after them by the raising of messengers like them.

It is said: The intended meaning is the raising of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and it is narrated from him that he said: "I am the prayer of my father Abraham." This is supported by the naming of his religion in several places in the Quran as "the creed of Abraham" (ملة إبراهيم). The meaning of the verse then returns to the meaning of His saying, narrating from Abraham and Ishmael during the building of the Kaaba: رَبَّنا وَاجْعَلنا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِنا أُمَّةً مُسْلِمَةً لَكَ — "Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You" (2:128) until He said: رَبَّنا وَابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولاً مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِكَ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ — "Our Lord, raise amongst them an apostle from among them, who should recite to them Your signs, and teach them the Book and wisdom, and purify them" (2:129).

It is also said: The intended meaning is that Allah grant him a beautiful remembrance and good praise after him until the Day of Resurrection. Allah indeed answered his prayer, for the followers of [various] religions praise him and mention him with beauty.

However, the application of "truthful tongue" (لسان صدق) to "beautiful remembrance" (الذكر الجميل) is somewhat obscure, as is the claim that this prayer and the one narrated in Surah al-Baqarah are one and the same.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاجْعَلْنِي مِنْ وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ النَّعِيمِ — "and make me one of the heirs of the Garden of Bliss" (26:85). The meaning of inheriting the Garden has already passed in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْوارِثُونَ — "It is they who will be the heirs" (23:10).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ — "and forgive my father, for he was one of those who are astray" (26:86). This is a plea for forgiveness for his father according to what he promised him in His saying: سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي — "Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you" (19:47). It is not far-fetched to infer from His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ — "Abraham’s pleading forgiveness for his father was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disassociated himself from him" (9:114), that he prayed for his father with this prayer while he was still alive. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ is that he had been among the people of straying for some time before the prayer.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — "and do not disgrace me on the day they will be resurrected, the day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:87-89). Disgrace (الخزي) is the lack of aid from one from whom aid is expected. The pronoun in يُبْعَثُونَ ("they will be resurrected") refers to people, and the lack of a prior mention does not harm since it is known from context.

It is known from his petition not to be disgraced on the Day of Resurrection that man is in need of divine aid on that day; for this weak structure cannot stand before the horrors it will face on the Day of Resurrection except by aid and support from Him, the Exalted.

His saying: يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ — the adverbial phrase is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ. By this, the claim of those who say that Abraham's speech ended at يُبْعَثُونَ and that the verses until the end of fifteen verses are the speech of Allah is refuted.

The verse negates the benefit of wealth and children on the Day of Resurrection. This is because the bond of wealth and children, which is the basis for mutual aid and support in this world, is an illusory social bond (رابطة وهمية اجتماعية) that has no effect in reality outside the context of civil society. The Day of Resurrection is the day of the uncovering of realities and the severing of causes (تقطع الأسباب); thus, wealth does not benefit by its "wealth-ness," nor do children by the relationship of their "son-ship" and kinship. He, the Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا فُرادى كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُمْ ما خَوَّلْناكُمْ وَراءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ — "Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time, and you have left behind your backs whatever We had bestowed on you" (6:94). And He said: فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَلا يَتَساءَلُونَ — "When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties of kinship between them on that day, nor will they ask [about] one another" (23:101).

Thus, the intended meaning of negating the benefit of wealth and children on the Day of Resurrection is the negation of their conventional and nominal causality (سببيتهما الوضعية الاعتبارية) in human society in this world. For wealth is a fine cause and means in society to attain vital objectives, and likewise, children are a fine means for strength, honor, victory, and power. Wealth and children are the primary things upon which man relies and to which he clings in the life of this world. Thus, negating their benefit on the Day of Resurrection is like a metonymy for negating the benefit of every conventional and nominal cause in human society used to seek material benefits, such as knowledge, craft, beauty, and others.

In other words, the negation of their benefit is in the sense of informing about the nullification of civil society along with the conventional and nominal causes that operate within it, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ بَلْ هُمُ الْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ — "'What is the matter with you that you do not help one another?' Rather today they are [wholly] submissive" (37:25-26).

His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ — al-Raghib said: "Peace (السلم) and soundness (السلامة) are to be free from outward and inward afflictions." End quote. The context suggests that he (عليه‌السلام) is in the station of mentioning a comprehensive meaning by which that Day is distinguished from others. He first asked his Lord to aid him and not disgrace him on a day when what used to benefit him in the world—wealth and children—will not benefit him. The requirement of this preamble is that the objective of His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ is to clarify what is beneficial on that day, and he mentioned therein the bringing of a sound heart.

Thus, the exception is disconnected (منقطع), and the meaning is: "But he who comes to Allah with a sound heart, he shall benefit from it." The result is that the pivot of felicity on that day is the soundness of the heart, whether its possessor had wealth and children in the world or not.

It is said: The exception is connected (متصل), and the excepted-from is the omitted object of "benefit" (ينفع), the estimation being: "the day when neither wealth nor children will benefit anyone except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart."

It is also said: The exception is connected and the speech assumes a suppressed possessive (مضاف), the estimation being: "neither wealth nor children will benefit except the wealth and children of him who comes..."

It is also said: Wealth and children are in the sense of "richness" (الغنى), and the exception from it is by suppressing a possessive of its kind, the estimation being: "the day when no richness will benefit except the richness of him who comes to Allah with a sound heart," and the soundness of the heart is a type of richness; thus, the exception is connected by way of claim, not reality.

It is also said: The exception is disconnected and there is a suppressed possessive, the estimation being: "neither wealth nor children will benefit except the state of him who comes..."

The first three views necessitate that the distinction of that Day be restricted only to those who possess wealth and children; for the speech according to them is in the sense of our saying: "the day when wealth and children will not benefit their owners except the sound-hearted among them," while those who have no wealth or children are left unmentioned, and the context does not support this. As for the fourth view, it is based on an estimation for which there is no need.

The verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: الْمالُ وَالْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَالْباقِياتُ الصَّالِحاتُ خَيْرٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ثَواباً وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلاً — "Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world, but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope" (18:46), except that it attributes benefit to the sound heart (القلب السليم), which is the soul (النفس) that is sound from the blemish of injustice (الظلم), which is polytheism and sin, as He, the Exalted, said in describing that Day: وَعَنَتِ الْوُجُوهُ لِلْحَيِّ الْقَيُّومِ وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ حَمَلَ ظُلْماً — "All faces shall be humbled before the Living One, the All-sustainer, and whoever carries [the burden of] injustice will be a failure" (20:111).

Some have said: "In the two verses there is support for the fact that his (عليه‌السلام) pleading for forgiveness for his father was a request for his guidance to faith, because of the impossibility of requesting forgiveness for him after his death as a disbeliever while knowing its lack of benefit, as it would be of the nature of intercession (الشفاعة)." End quote.

This—on the assumption of taking the exception as connected as this speaker held—is based on Abraham (عليه‌السلام) being the biological son of Azar, and the invalidity of this view has already passed in the story of Abraham in Surah al-An'am, where the verses are explicit to the contrary.

However, if the exception is taken as disconnected, then His saying: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ, joined with His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى — "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (21:28), is evidence that the pleading for forgiveness was before his death, as is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِلْغاوِينَ — "The Garden will be brought near for the God-conscious, and the Hell will be laid open for the perverse" (26:90-91). الإزلاف is bringing near, and التبريز is making manifest. In the contrast between the God-conscious (المتقين) and the perverse (الغاوين), and the choice of these two descriptions for these two groups, there is an indication of what Allah, the Glorified, decreed on the day Iblis was stoned when he refused to prostrate to Adam, as mentioned in Surah al-Hijr: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمَوْعِدُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you. Indeed hell is the tryst of them all" (15:42-43), until He said: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ — "Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs" (15:45).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ هَلْ يَنْصُرُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَنْتَصِرُونَ — "and they will be asked, 'Where is that which you used to worship besides Allah? Do they help you, or do they help themselves?'" (26:92-93); meaning, do they avert misery and punishment from you or from themselves? The result is that it will become clear to them that they went astray in their worship of other than Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها هُمْ وَالْغاوُونَ وَجُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ أَجْمَعُونَ — "Then they will be cast into it—they and the perverse, and the hosts of Iblis, all together" (26:94-95). It is said: كبه فانكب (he threw him down and he fell on his face), and كبكبه means he threw him on his face time after time. Thus, it signifies the repetition of the act of throwing, like دب ودبدب (crept), ذب وذبذب (wavered), زل وزلزل (slipped/shook), and دك ودكدك (crushed).

The plural pronoun in His saying: فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها هُمْ refers to the idols, as indicated by His saying: إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ — "Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell" (21:98). These are one of the three groups that the verse mentions will be cast into Hell on the Day of Resurrection. The second group is the perverse (الغاوون) for whom that was decreed, as in the verse of al-Hijr quoted just now. The third group is the hosts of Iblis (جنود إبليس), who are the companions of the devils (قرناء الشياطين) whom the Quran mentions do not part from the people of perversity until they enter the Fire. He, the Exalted, said: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطاناً فَهو لَهُ قَرِينٌ — "Whoever turns a blind eye to the remembrance of the All-beneficent, We assign him a devil who remains his companion" (43:36), until He said: وَلَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ — "It will not avail you today, as you were unjust, that you are partners in the punishment" (43:39).

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا وَهُمْ فِيها يَخْتَصِمُونَ — "They will say, as they wrangle in it" (26:96) until His saying: إِلَّا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "except the guilty" (26:99). It is apparent that the speakers are the perverse, and the wrangling (الاختصام) occurs among them; they wrangle with themselves and with the devils, as Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned in several places in His speech.

His saying: تَاللهِ إِنْ كُنَّا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ — "By Allah, we were indeed in manifest error" (26:97) is a confession of error from them. The address in His saying: إِذْ نُسَوِّيكُمْ بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — "when we equated you with the Lord of all the worlds" (26:98) is to the deities among the idols who are with them in the Fire, or to them and the devils, or to both and the followed leaders among the perverse. The best of these views is the first.

His saying: وَما أَضَلَّنا إِلَّا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "and none led us astray except the guilty" (26:99). It is apparent that each of the speakers intends by "the guilty" (المجرمون) someone other than himself—such as an imam of misguidance whom he followed in the world, a caller who invited him to polytheism and he followed him, polytheistic fathers whom he imitated, or a close friend whom he resembled. The guilty (المجرمون), as inferred from the verses of the Resurrection, are those in whom guilt was established and for whom entry into the Fire was decreed. He, the Exalted, said: وَامْتازُوا الْيَوْمَ أَيُّهَا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — "And [a voice will say,] 'Get apart today, O you guilty ones!'" (36:59).

His saying, the Exalted: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَلا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ — "Now we have no intercessors, nor any sympathetic friend" (26:100-101). The حميم (sympathetic friend), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the close, compassionate one.

This speech is an expression of regret from them over their deprivation of the intercession of intercessors and the relief of friends. In the expression: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ ("we have no intercessors"), there is an indication of the existence of intercessors there who intercede for some of the sinners. Were it not for that, the speech would have properly been: "we have no intercessor" (شافع), as there is no point necessitating the plural. It has been narrated that they say this when they see the angels, the prophets, and the believers interceding.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Had there been another turn for us, we would have been among the believers" (26:102). This is a wish from them to return to the world so that they might be among the believers and thus attain the felicity that the believers attained.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً — "There is indeed a sign in that" (26:103) until the end of the two verses. That is, in the story of Abraham (عليه‌السلام) and his adherence, by his simple primordial nature, to the religion of monotheism, his turning his face toward the Lord of all the worlds, his disassociation from idols, and his argumentation against the idolaters and idol-worshippers, there is a sign for whoever reflects upon it. Furthermore, in the rest of his stories—his trials and tribulations not mentioned here, such as being cast into the fire, the arrival of the angelic guests, the story of his settling Ishmael and his mother in the valley of Mecca, the building of the Kaaba, and the sacrifice of Ishmael—there are signs for those of understanding.

His saying: وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "and most of them were not believers" (26:103); meaning, most of Abraham's people were not believers. The rest is clear from what has preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ, he said: "It is the Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام)."

I say: This is possible as an interpretation (تفسير) or as an application (جري).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Yahya, from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), he said: "The Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) said: 'A truthful mention (لسان الصدق) for a man that Allah places among the people is better than wealth that he consumes and leaves as inheritance.'" The tradition continues.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاغْفِرْ لِأَبِي, Abd ibn Humayd, ibn al-Mundhir, and ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Qatadah: "Regarding His saying: وَلا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ, he said: 'It was mentioned to us that the Prophet of Allah (ص) said: "A man from the believers will surely come on the Day of Resurrection, holding the hand of a polytheist father of his, until he crosses the Fire, hoping to enter him into the Garden. Then a caller will call out to him that no polytheist shall enter the Garden. He will say: 'My Lord, my father! And You promised me that You would not disgrace me.'"'

He said: 'He will continue to cling to him until Allah transforms him into an ugly form and a foul smell—in the form of a male hyena (ضبعان). When he sees him like that, he will disassociate himself from him and say: "You are not my father."' He said: 'So we used to see that he meant Abraham and what he was named with on that day.'"

In the same source, al-Bukhari and al-Nasa'i narrated from Abu Hurayrah, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "Abraham will meet his father Azar on the Day of Resurrection, and upon Azar's face will be darkness and dust. Abraham will say to him: 'Did I not tell you: Do not disobey me?' His father will say: 'Today I will not disobey you.'

Abraham will say: 'My Lord, You promised me that You would not disgrace me on the day they are resurrected; so what disgrace is more disgraceful than my father being far removed?' Allah will say: 'I have forbidden the Garden to the disbelievers.' Then it will be said: 'O Abraham, what is beneath your feet?' And behold, he will see a hyena (ذيخ) covered in filth; it will be taken by its legs and cast into the Fire."

I say: These two reports are among the reports of Abraham being the biological son of Azar. It has already passed in the stories of Abraham in Surah al-An'am that they contradict the Book, and His speech, the Exalted, is explicit to the contrary.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Sufyan ibn Uyaynah, he said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ. He said: 'The sound heart (السليم) is that which meets its Lord while there is no one in it besides Him.'

He said: 'Every heart in which there is polytheism (شرك) or doubt (شك) is fallen. They only intended by asceticism (الزهد) in the world to free their hearts for the Hereafter.'"

In al-Majma', it is narrated from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) that he said: "It is the heart that is sound from the love of the world." This is supported by the saying of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Love of the world is the head of every fault."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Salim, from Abu Ja'far (عليه‌السلام): In a tradition regarding وَجُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ أَجْمَعُونَ, he said: "The hosts of Iblis are his progeny from the devils."

He said: "And their saying: وَما أَضَلَّنا إِلَّا الْمُجْرِمُونَ — 'when they invited us to their path' — that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, regarding them when He gathers them to the Fire: قالَتْ أُخْراهُمْ لِأُولاهُمْ رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ أَضَلُّونا فَآتِهِمْ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً مِنَ النَّارِ — 'The last of them will say about the first of them, "Our Lord, these led us astray; so give them a double punishment of the Fire"' (7:38). And His saying: كُلَّما دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَعَنَتْ أُخْتَها حَتَّى إِذَا ادَّارَكُوا فِيها جَمِيعاً — 'Every time a nation enters, it will curse its sister [nation], until when they have all gathered in it' (7:38), they will disassociate from one another and curse one another. Some of them will want to argue against others hoping for success, so that they might all escape the greatness of what has befallen them. But it is not a time of trial or testing, nor of the acceptance of excuses, nor a time of salvation."

In al-Kafi as well, with two chains from Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها هُمْ وَالْغاوُونَ, he said: "They are a people who described justice with their tongues and then contradicted it for something else."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir, and al-Barqi in al-Mahasin, from Abu Abdillah (ع). It is apparent that the narration was originally regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ — "As for the poets, it is the perverse who follow them" (26:224), because of what follows it: وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ ما لا يَفْعَلُونَ — "and that they say what they do not do" (26:226). An error occurred in placing it under His saying: فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها, etc., as is clear to the reflective reader.

In al-Majma', in the tradition narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah, he said: "I heard the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'A man in the Garden will say: "What has my friend done?" while his friend is in the Hellfire. Allah will say: "Bring out his friend for him to the Garden." Then those remaining in the Fire will say: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَلا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ — "Now we have no intercessors, nor any sympathetic friend."'"

It is narrated with a chain from Humran ibn A'yan, from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: "By Allah, we shall surely intercede for our followers (شيعتنا) three times until the people say: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَلا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ until His saying: فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ." In another narration: "until our enemy says [it]."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ, he said: "From the guided (المهتدين)." He said: "Because faith (الإيمان) has become necessary for them by confession."

I say: His meaning is that they believe on that day with the faith of certainty (إيمان إيقان), but they see that faith on that day does not benefit them. Rather, the beneficial faith is faith in the world. Thus, they wish to return to the world so that their faith might be of the faith of the guided—who are the true believers guided by their faith on the Day of Resurrection. This is a subtle meaning, and to it points His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ تَرى إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا فَارْجِعْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً إِنَّا مُوقِنُونَ — "Were you to see when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [confessing], 'Our Lord, we have seen and heard; so send us back [to the world] that we may act righteously. Indeed we [now] have certainty'" (32:12). They did not say, "Send us back so we may believe and act righteously," but rather they said, "Send us back so we may act righteously." Understand that.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٠٥)
The people of Noah impugned the apostles (105)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٠٦)
when Noah, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (106)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٠٧)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (107)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٠٨)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (108)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٠٩)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (109)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١١٠)
So be wary of Allah and obey me.’ (110)
۞ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَٱتَّبَعَكَ ٱلْأَرْذَلُونَ (١١١)
They said, ‘Shall we believe in you, when it is the riffraff who follow you?’ (111)
قَالَ وَمَا عِلْمِى بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١١٢)
He said, ‘What do I know as to what they used to do? (112)
إِنْ حِسَابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّى ۖ لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ (١١٣)
Their reckoning is only with my Lord, should you be aware. (113)
وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٤)
I will not drive away the faithful. (114)
إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١١٥)
I am just a manifest warner.’ (115)
قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰنُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَرْجُومِينَ (١١٦)
They said, ‘Noah, if you do not desist, you will certainly be stoned [to death].’ (116)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِى كَذَّبُونِ (١١٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my people have impugned me. (117)
فَٱفْتَحْ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا وَنَجِّنِى وَمَن مَّعِىَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٨)
So judge conclusively between me and them, and deliver me and the faithful who are with me.’ (118)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (١١٩)
Thereupon We delivered him and those who were with him in the laden ark. (119)
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا بَعْدُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ (١٢٠)
Then We drowned the rest. (120)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٢١)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (121)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٢٢)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (122)

(Commentary)

After concluding the accounts of Moses and Abraham (peace be upon them)—both of whom were among the Arch-Prophets (أولو العزم) — the elite messengers endowed with constancy and a divinely ordained law—the verses now point toward the story of Noah (peace be upon him). He was the first of the Arch-Prophets and the masters of the prophets. The verses summarize what transpired between him and his people: most of them did not believe in him, so Allah drowned them and delivered Noah and the believers who were with him.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "The people of Noah impugned the apostles." (26:105). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The term 'people' (القوم) originally referred to a group of men to the exclusion of women; hence His saying: لا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَوْمٍ 'Let not any people ridicule another people' (49:11). A poet once said: 'Are they the people of the House of Hisn, or are they women?' However, in the general usage of the Quran, it is intended to include both men and women together." [End of quote].

It is said that the word 'people' (القوم) is masculine, and the feminization of the verb attributed to it is based on the interpretation of it as a 'group' (الجماعة). Others say it is feminine, while it is stated in al-Misbah that it can be treated as either masculine or feminine.

The people are described as having impugned "the apostles" (المرسلين) in the plural, even though they only rejected one of them, namely Noah (peace be upon him). This is because the call of the messengers is one, and their word is united upon monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Therefore, whoever rejects one of them has effectively rejected them all. For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, has deemed faith in some messengers while rejecting others as actual disbelief in all of them. He, the Exalted, says: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا "Those who deny Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and deny others,’ and seek to take a way in between—it is they who are truly faithless." (4:150-151).

It has also been suggested that this is similar to the Arabic expression: "So-and-so rides beasts (الدواب) and wears cloaks (البرود)," even if he possesses only one beast and one cloak. In this sense, the plural serves as a metaphor for the genus. However, the first explanation is more appropriate. A similar logic applies to the upcoming verses: كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "The ‘Ad impugned the apostles" (26:123) and كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "The Thamud impugned the apostles" (26:141), and others.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ "When Noah, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be God-wary?" (26:106). The word "brother" (الأخ) here refers to a kinsman, as in the expressions "a brother of Tamim" or "a brother of Kulayb." The interrogative form is intended as a rebuke.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ "I am indeed a trusted apostle to you." (26:107). That is, an apostle from Allah, the Glorified, who is trustworthy (أمين) regarding the message I have been tasked to carry; I convey to you nothing except what my Lord has commanded me and what He desires from you. For this reason, He branched from this His saying: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ "So be wary of Allah and obey me." (26:108). He commanded them to obey him because obedience to him is obedience to Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds." (26:109). This is phrased to negate any worldly greed by denying the request for a wage (الأجر). This proves that he is a sincere advisor to them in what he calls them toward, neither betraying them nor deceiving them. Therefore, they must obey him in what he commands. Consequently, He branched from this a second time His saying: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ "So be wary of Allah and obey me." (26:110).

The shift in His saying: إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds" from the Majestic Name (Allah) to "the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين) is to explicitly indicate monotheism (التوحيد). This is because they believed that He, the Exalted, was the God of the world of gods, yet they believed that every world had another god whom they worshipped besides Allah. Thus, affirming Him, the Exalted, as the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين) collectively is an explicit declaration of the oneness of worship and an absolute negation of any deities besides Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ "So be wary of Allah and obey me." (26:110). The reason for the repetition of this verse has been mentioned previously; it signifies that both trustworthiness (الأمانة) and the absence of asking for a reward are each independent reasons necessitating their obedience to him.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ "They said, ‘Shall we believe in you when it is the lowliest who follow you?’" (26:111). The "lowliest" (الأرذلون) is the plural of ardhal (أرذل), which is a superlative noun derived from radhalah (الرذالة), meaning vileness and meanness. Their intent in describing his followers as "lowly" was that they were people of vile deeds and mean occupations. This is why he (peace be upon him) responded with: وَما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "What do I know as to what they used to do?" (26:112).

It appears that they viewed nobility and honor as residing in wealth and the multitude of sons and followers, as can be inferred from the supplication of Noah (peace be upon him) when he said: رَبِّ إِنَّهُمْ عَصَوْنِي وَاتَّبَعُوا مَنْ لَمْ يَزِدْهُ مالُهُ وَوَلَدُهُ إِلَّا خَساراً "My Lord! They have disobeyed me and followed someone whose wealth and children only add to his loss." (71:21). Thus, by "the lowliest," they meant those whom the nobles and the affluent considered the rabble—slaves, the poor, and practitioners of lowly crafts—whose company they avoided.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ وَما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "He said, ‘What do I know as to what they used to do?" (26:112). The pronoun refers to Noah (peace be upon him). The word "what" (ما) is interrogative, though some say it is negatory, in which case the predicate is omitted as indicated by the context. In any case, the intent is to negate his knowledge of their deeds prior to their believing in him, due to the phrase: كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "they used to do."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ حِسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ "Their reckoning is only with my Lord, if you use your reason." (26:113). The intent of His saying "my Lord" (ربي) is the Lord of all the worlds, for He was the One Whom Noah specifically called them toward. His saying: لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ "if you use your reason" is detached from the preceding grammatical action, meaning: "if only you possessed perception." It has also been said to mean: "if you perceived anything, you would have known that," but this is weak.

The meaning, in light of the restriction at the beginning of the verse, is: "I have no knowledge of their past deeds, nor is it my responsibility to reckon them such that I should spy on or investigate their actions. Rather, their reckoning is only with my Lord—if only you perceived—and He will recompense them according to their deeds."

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ "I will not drive away the believers. I am just a manifest warner." (26:114-115).
