The second verse serves as a justification for the first, and the whole completes the previous explanation. The meaning is: "My only role is warning and invitation; I shall not drive away whoever approaches me and believes in me, nor shall I scrutinize their past deeds to hold them to account, for their reckoning is with my Lord—the Lord of all the worlds—and not with me."

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يا نُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمَرْجُومِينَ "They said, ‘If you do not desist, O Noah, you will surely be stoned.’" (26:116). Desisting (الانتهاء) here means ceasing the call. Stoning (الرجم) is the act of pelting with stones. It has been suggested that it means revilement, but this is far-fetched. This was said toward the end of his period of calling them, as they threatened him with a decisive statement, evidenced by the various modes of emphasis in the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي كَذَّبُونِ فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحاً "He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my people have impugned me. So judge between me and them with a [decisive] judgment..." (26:117-118). This is a plea for a decisive opening (استفتاح) from him (peace be upon him). He prefaced it with his saying: رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي كَذَّبُونِ "My Lord! Indeed my people have impugned me" as a preamble—meaning that their absolute rejection has been realized, leaving no hope for their belief. This is further understood from his supplication against them: رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً "My Lord! Do not leave on the earth any inhabitant from among the faithless. If You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates." (71:26-27).

His saying: فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحاً "So judge between me and them with a [decisive] judgment" is a metaphor for the decree (القضاء) between him and his people, as Allah says: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is an apostle for every nation; so when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged." (10:47).

The origin of this is a metaphorical borrowing (استعارة بالكناية); it is as if he, his followers, and the disbelievers among his people were mixed and gathered without distinction, so he asked his Lord to "open" (يفتح) between them by creating a space between him and his people, thereby distancing one group from the other. This is a metaphor for the descent of punishment, for none are destroyed except the transgressing people. The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: وَنَجِّنِي وَمَنْ مَعِيَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "and deliver me and the believers who are with me." (26:118).

It has also been said that "opening" (الفتح) means ruling and judging, derived from al-fatahah (الفتاحة), meaning governance.

His saying, the Exalted: فأَنْجَيْناهُ وَمَنْ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ "So We delivered him and those who were with him in the laden ark." (26:119). That is, the ark filled with them and with a pair of every kind, as mentioned in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنا بَعْدُ الْباقِينَ "Then We drowned the rest thereafter." (26:120). Meaning, We drowned the remainder of his people after delivering them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً "There is indeed a sign in that..." until His saying: الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (26:121-122). The discussion regarding the meaning of these two verses has preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the book Kamal al-Din and Rawdat al-Kafi, it is narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "Noah remained for a thousand years less fifty—no one shared in his prophethood—but he came to a people who had rejected the prophets who were between him and Adam. That is the meaning of the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ 'The people of Noah impugned the apostles,' meaning those who were between him and Adam," until the end of the tradition.

It is also stated therein: "There were ten ancestors between him and Adam, all of whom were prophets." In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ "when it is the lowliest who follow you," he said: "The poor."

In the same source, and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ "the laden ark," he said: "The equipped [ark] that had been completed and lacked nothing but to be launched."

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
[The people of] ‘Ad impugned the apostles, (123)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
when Hud, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (124)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٢٥)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (125)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٢٦)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (126)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢٧)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (127)
أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ ءَايَةً تَعْبَثُونَ (١٢٨)
Do you build futile a sign on every prominence? (128)
وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ (١٢٩)
You set up structures as if you will be immortal, (129)
وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ (١٣٠)
and when you seize [someone for punishment], you seize [him] like tyrants. (130)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٣١)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (131)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَمَدَّكُم بِمَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٣٢)
Be wary of Him who has provided you with whatever you know, (132)
أَمَدَّكُم بِأَنْعَـٰمٍ وَبَنِينَ (١٣٣)
and aided you with sons and with cattle, (133)
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٣٤)
gardens and springs. (134)
إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٣٥)
Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (135)
قَالُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْوَٰعِظِينَ (١٣٦)
They said, ‘It is the same to us whether you exhort us or not. (136)
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا خُلُقُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣٧)
These are nothing but the traditions of the ancients, (137)
وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (١٣٨)
and we will not be punished.’ (138)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٩)
So they impugned him, whereupon We destroyed them. There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (139)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٤٠)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (140)

(Commentary)

The verses refer to the story of Hud (عليه السلام) and his people, who were the people of ‘Ad.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ "‘Ad impugned the apostles" (26:123). The people of ‘Ad were from the first of the "Arabized" Arabs (العرب العاربة الأولى). They inhabited al-Ahqaf in the Arabian Peninsula and possessed a sophisticated civilization, fertile lands, and populated dwellings. However, they denied the messengers, were ungrateful for the blessings of Allah, and transgressed. Consequently, Allah destroyed them with a "barren wind" (الريح العقيم) — a devastating wind that brings no benefit or rain — ruined their homes, and effaced their traces.

It is said that ‘Ad was the name of their forefather; thus, naming the tribe after him is similar to naming a people after their ancestor, such as saying Tamim, Bakr, or Taghlib, meaning the children of Tamim, Bakr, and Taghlib.

The reason for considering the people as deniers of "the apostles" (المرسلين) — even though they outwardly only denied one of them — has already been discussed in the parallel verse regarding the story of Nuh.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ "Indeed I am a trusted apostle to you" (26:125) — up to His saying — رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "Lord of all the worlds" (26:127). The discussion regarding these verses has preceded in their counterparts within the story of Nuh (عليه السلام).

Some commentators have mentioned that prefacing these five stories by mentioning the trustworthiness of the messengers, their refusal to ask for a wage for their mission, and their command to the people toward godwariness (التقوى) and obedience (الطاعة) is to highlight that the foundation of the prophetic mission (البعثة) is the call to the knowledge of the Truth and obedience in that which brings the invited closer to reward and distances them from punishment. It also shows that the prophets (عليه السلام) are united upon this, even if they differ in some branches of the various divine laws (الشرائع) which vary according to times and eras, and that they are entirely purified from worldly ambitions.

A similar point applies to the conclusion of all seven stories mentioned in this Surah with His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (26:139-140). This indicates that most nations and peoples turn away from the signs of Allah, and that Allah, the Glorified, is All-mighty (عزيز) — possessing absolute power and invincibility — and thus He punishes them for their denial, yet He is All-merciful (رحيم) and saves the believers through His mercy. This was previously indicated in the discussion regarding the objective of the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ "Do you build a monument on every high ground vainly?" (26:128). The ri‘ (الريع) is a high place on the earth, a ayah (الآية) is a mark or monument (العلامة), and ‘abath (العبث) is an action that has no purposeful end. It seems they used to build structures like landmarks on the peaks of mountains and every elevated ground for the sake of recreation and boasting, without any necessity driving them to do so, but rather out of idle play and following whims (الهوى); thus, he rebuked them for it.

Other meanings have been mentioned for this verse, but they lack evidence from the wording and do not suit the context, so we have set them aside.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ "and take up [stately] castles as if you were to live forever?" (26:129). The masani‘ (المصانع), according to what has been said, are fortified strongholds, lofty palaces, and high buildings; the singular is masna‘ (مصنع).

His saying: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ "as if you were to live forever" is in the position of explaining the cause (التعليل) for what preceded it. That is: you take up these strongholds because you hope for immortality (الخلود); were it not for the hope of immortality, you would not have performed such works which, by their nature, are meant to endure for long ages that the longest of human lifespans cannot fulfill. Other views regarding the meaning of the verse and its individual words have been mentioned, which we have overlooked.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بَطَشْتُمْ بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ "And when you seize [someone], you seize [him] like tyrants" (26:130). It is said in al-Majma‘: "Seizing (البطش) is oppression through killing with the sword or striking with the whip. The tyrant (الجبار) is one who is exalted over others by his great power. As an attribute of Allah, the Glorified, it is praise, but as an attribute of others, it is blame, because for a servant it means he affects a state of forced compulsion (الجبرية)."

The meaning is: when you manifest intensity in action and prowess, you exceed all bounds in that, just as tyrants exceed bounds in their severity.

The sum of these three verses is that you are extravagant (مسرفون) in both the aspects of desire (الشهوة) and anger (الغضب), transgressing the limits of moderation and departing from the state of servitude (العبودية).

His saying, the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ "So be wary of Allah and obey me" (26:131). This is a derivation (تفريع) from their extravagance in desire and anger and their departure from the state of servitude. Therefore, let them be godwary of Allah and obey him in what he commands them regarding the abandonment of luxury (الإتراف) and arrogance (الاستكبار).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ "Be wary of Him who has aided you with what you know" (26:132) — up to His saying — وَعُيُونٍ "and springs" (26:134). Al-Raghib said: "The root of madd (المد) is pulling or drawing. He said: 'I aided (أمددت) the army with reinforcements (بمدود) and the person with food.' He noted: 'Most often, imdad (الإمداد) is used for what is liked, while madd (المد) is used for what is disliked.' Allah says: وَأَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ 'and We shall provide them with fruits' (52:22) and وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا 'and We shall prolong for him the punishment' (19:79)."

His saying: وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي أَمَدَّكُمْ "Be wary of Him who has aided you..." etc., is in the sense of linking a ruling to a description that suggests causality (العلية). That is: be godwary of Allah who aids you with His blessings, for since He aids you with them, it is obligatory upon you to thank Him by placing His blessings in their proper place without luxury or arrogance; for ingratitude toward blessings (كفران النعمة) entails wrath and punishment. Allah says: لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ "If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe" (14:7).

He mentioned the blessings generally by saying first: أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ "He has aided you with what you know," then He detailed them by saying second: أَمَدَّكُمْ بِأَنْعامٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ "He has aided you with cattle and sons, and gardens and springs" (26:133-134).

In His saying: أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ "He has aided you with what you know," there is another subtle point (نكتة): that you know these blessings are from His aid and His making, and no one else shares with Him in creating them or providing them. Therefore, He is the One whom you must be godwary of through gratitude and worship (العبادة), rather than idols and statues. Thus, the speech contains a logical proof (الحجة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ "Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day" (26:135). This is the reason for the command toward godwariness. That is: I command you toward godwariness out of gratitude because I fear for you the punishment of a great day if you disbelieve and are not grateful. It appears that the "great day" refers to the Day of Resurrection, although some have allowed that it might refer to the day of the punishment of extermination (عذاب الاستئصال).

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ "They said, ‘It is the same to us whether you advise [us] or do not happen to be one of the advisers’" (26:136). This is a denial of the effect of his speech and a total despairing of their faith.

It is said: the speech is not devoid of hyperbole (مبالغة). The requirement of the alternative would have been to say: "whether you advise or do not advise." In turning away from that to His saying: أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ "or do not happen to be one of the advisers," which denies his very status as an adviser, there is an obvious hyperbole.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا خُلُقُ الْأَوَّلِينَ "This is nothing but the custom of the ancients" (26:137). The word khuluq (الخلق) is with a damma on the kha and lam, or with a sukun on the lam. Al-Raghib said: "Khalq (الخلق) and khuluq (الخلق) — with the kha having a fatha or a damma — are originally the same, like sharb and shurb. However, khalq (with fatha) is specialized for forms and shapes perceived by sight, while khuluq (with damma) is specialized for powers and dispositions (السجايا) perceived by insight (البصيرة). Allah says: إِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ 'and indeed you possess a great character' (68:4). And it is also recited as: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا خُلُقُ الْأَوَّلِينَ."

The demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to what Hud brought, which they called "advice" (وعظ). The meaning is: this call to monotheism (التوحيد) and this advice that you are engaged in is nothing but the habit of the first humans of the past, who were people of myths and superstitions. This is like their saying: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ "This is nothing but the myths of the ancients" (6:25).

It is also possible that "this" refers to their own state of polytheism (الشرك) and the worship of deities besides Allah, following their first forefathers, as in their saying: وَجَدْنا آباءَنا كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ "We found our fathers doing so" (26:74).

Some have suggested the meaning to be: "our creation is nothing but the creation of the ancients; we live as they lived and die as they died, and there is no resurrection, no reckoning, and no punishment." This is far-fetched given the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ "and we will not be punished" (26:138). This is a denial of the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and life after death — based on the "great day" in the speech of Hud (عليه السلام) referring to the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً "So they impugned him, and We destroyed them. There is indeed a sign in that..." (26:139) — up to His saying — الرَّحِيمُ "the All-merciful" (26:140). Its meaning is clear from what has preceded.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the book Kamal al-Din and Rawdat al-Kafi, it is narrated with a chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja‘far Muhammad bin ‘Ali al-Baqir (عليه السلام) in a tradition: "And Nuh said: 'Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, is sending a prophet called Hud. He will call his people to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, but they will deny him. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will destroy them with the wind. So whoever among you reaches him, let him believe in him and follow him, for Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, will save him from the punishment of the wind.'"

"And Nuh commanded his son Shem to observe this testament at the beginning of every year, and it would be a day of celebration (عيد) for them, wherein they would observe the sending of Hud and the time in which he would appear."

"When Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, sent Hud, they looked into the knowledge and faith they possessed, the inheritance of knowledge, the Greatest Name (الاسم الأكبر), and the traces of the knowledge of prophethood. They found Hud to be a prophet whom their father Nuh had given glad tidings of. So they believed in him, testified to his truth, and followed him, and they were saved from the punishment of the wind. This is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً 'And to ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother' (7:65) and His saying: كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ ـ إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلا تَتَّقُونَ '‘Ad impugned the apostles, when Hud, their brother, said to them, "Will you not be wary [of Allah]?"' (26:123-124)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ "a monument vainly": "Meaning that which you do not need for your dwelling, but you only intend vanity, play, and diversion by it. It is as if he considered their building of what they could do without as vanity on their part." This is from Ibn ‘Abbas in the narration of ‘Ata’. It is supported by the reported tradition from Anas bin Malik: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out and saw a dome (قبة) and said: 'What is this?' His companions said to him: 'This belongs to a man from the Ansar.' He remained until the owner came and greeted the people, but the Prophet turned away from him. He did this several times until the man recognized the anger and the turning away."

"He complained about this to his companions and said: 'By Allah, I find the look of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) strange; I do not know what has happened regarding me or what I have done?' They said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out and saw your dome and asked: "To whom does this belong?" and we informed him.' So the man returned to his dome and leveled it to the ground. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) went out one day and did not see the dome, so he said: 'What happened to the dome that was here?' They said: 'Its owner complained to us of your turning away from him, so we informed him and he demolished it.'"

"He said: 'Indeed, everything that is built is a burden (وبال) upon its owner on the Day of Resurrection, except that which is indispensable.'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا بَطَشْتُمْ بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ "And when you seize [someone], you seize [him] like tyrants", he said: "You kill out of anger without justification."

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٤١)
[The people of] Thamud impugned the apostles (141)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ صَـٰلِحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٤٢)
when Salih, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (142)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٤٣)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (143)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٤٤)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (144)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٤٥)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (145)
أَتُتْرَكُونَ فِى مَا هَـٰهُنَآ ءَامِنِينَ (١٤٦)
Will you be left secure in that which is here (146)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٤٧)
—amid gardens and springs, (147)
وَزُرُوعٍ وَنَخْلٍ طَلْعُهَا هَضِيمٌ (١٤٨)
farms and date palms with dainty spathes (148)
وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا فَـٰرِهِينَ (١٤٩)
—while you skillfully hew out houses from the mountains? (149)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٥٠)
So be wary of Allah and obey me, (150)
وَلَا تُطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (١٥١)
and do not obey the dictates of the transgressors, (151)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ (١٥٢)
who cause corruption in the land and do not bring about reform.’ (152)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (١٥٣)
They said, ‘Indeed you are one of the bewitched. (153)
مَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا فَأْتِ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١٥٤)
You are just a human being like us. So bring us a sign, should you be truthful.’ (154)
قَالَ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةٌ لَّهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (١٥٥)
He said, ‘This is a she-camel; she shall drink and you shall drink on known days. (155)
وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥٦)
Do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by the punishment of a terrible day.’ (156)
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ نَـٰدِمِينَ (١٥٧)
But they hamstrung her, whereupon they became regretful. (157)
فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٥٨)
So the punishment seized them. There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (158)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٥٩)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (159)

(Commentary)

The verses point toward a summary of the story of Salih (عليه السلام) and his people. He is one of the Arab prophets and is mentioned in the Qur’an after Hud (عليه السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ (The [people of] Thamud contradicted the apostles) until His saying: عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (from the Lord of all the worlds). Their meaning has become clear from what has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتُتْرَكُونَ فِي ما هاهُنا آمِنِينَ (Will you be left secure in what is here?) [26:146]. It is apparent that the interrogation is for the purpose of denunciation (الإنكار). The word "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun referring to the blessings (النعم) which He details after His saying: فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (in gardens and springs) etc. "Here" (هاهنا) is a demonstrative referring to the present, nearby location, which is the land of Thamud. "Secure" (آمنين) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pro-agent of "you be left" (تتركون).

The meaning is: You will not be left in these blessings that surround you in this land of yours, while you are given free rein, not being questioned about what you do, secure from any divine reckoning (مؤاخذة إلهية).

His saying, the Exalted: فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ وَزُرُوعٍ وَنَخْلٍ طَلْعُها هَضِيمٌ (in gardens and springs, and cornfields and date palms with succulent spathes?) [26:147-148]. This is a detailed exposition (بيان تفصيلي) of His saying: "in what is here" (في ما هاهنا). The date palm was specifically mentioned despite being included in "gardens" due to their great concern for it. The "spathe" (الطلع) in the date palm is like the blossom in other trees, and "succulent" (الهضيم) — according to what has been said — refers to that which is overlapping and tightly packed together.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً فارِهِينَ (and you hew out houses from the mountains skillfully?) [26:149]. Al-Raghib said: "Al-Farah (الفره) — with a fatha then a kasra as an adjective — means 'insolent' (الأشر). His saying, the Exalted: وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً فارِهِينَ means 'skillfully' (حاذقين), and it is said it means 'insolently' (أشرين)." End of summary. Based on what he chose, the verse would be an exposition of the blessing; based on the other meaning, it would be intended to denounce their insolence and arrogance (بطرهم). In any case, the verse falls within the scope of the interrogation.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (So be wary of Allah and obey me) [26:150]. This is a derivation from the preceding denunciation, which carries the sense of a negation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا يُصْلِحُونَ (and do not obey the bid of the profligate, who cause corruption in the land and do not set things right) [26:151-152]. It is apparent that "bid" or "command" (الأمر) here refers to the opposite of "prohibition" (النهي), evidenced by the prohibition of obeying it. Some have allowed for "command" (الأمر) to mean "affair" or "state" (الشأن), in which case the meaning of obeying their command would be the commoners' imitation (تقليد) and following of them in their actions and their treading of the paths they wish for them to tread.

In any case, the "profligate" (المسرفين) — those who exceed the bounds of moderation and truth — refers to the nobles and grandees of the people who are followed, and the address is to the commoners who follow them. As for the masters and nobles, there was no hope for their faith or their following of the truth.

It is possible that the address is to everyone, since the nobles among them also used to imitate their forefathers and obey their command, as they said to Salih (عليه السلام): أَتَنْهانا أَنْ نَعْبُدَ ما يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا (Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers used to worship?) [11:62]. Thus, they were all obeying the command of the profligate, so they were forbidden from it.

He explained the "profligate" (المسرفين) — who are those who transgress against the truth and depart from the limit of moderation — by describing them with His saying: الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا يُصْلِحُونَ (who cause corruption in the land and do not set things right). This is an indication of the actual cause (علة) of the ruling. The meaning is: Be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and do not obey the command of the profligate because they are corrupters in the land, not reformers, and with corruption (الإفساد), one is not safe from divine punishment, for He is All-Mighty, Possessor of Retribution.

This is because the cosmos (الكون), despite the opposition and conflict (تزاحم) between its parts, is composed with a specific composition through which its parts harmonize with one another in their results and effects. This is like the case of the two pans of a scale; despite their agitation and extreme difference in rising and falling, they are in agreement in determining the weight of the goods being weighed, which is the goal. The human world, which is a part of the cosmos, is likewise. Furthermore, the individual human being, with his various and opposing powers and instruments, is created with a primordial nature (مفطور) to moderate his actions and deeds such that each of his powers receives its predestined share. He has been equipped with an intellect (عقل) that distinguishes between good and evil and gives every possessor of a right his due.

Thus, the cosmos proceeds by the system (النظام) current within it toward righteous, intended goals. Through the complete interconnection between its parts, it delineates for each of its parts a specific path in which it proceeds with specific actions, without deviating from its very center to the right or left, and without swerving through excess (إفراط) or deficiency (تفريط). Indeed, in deviation and swerving there is a corruption (إفساد) of the prescribed system, followed by the corruption of its goal and the goal of the whole. It is necessary that the departure of some parts from the line delineated for them, and the corruption of the order (النظم) imposed for them and for others, entails the contention of the remaining parts against them. If they are able to straighten them and return them to the center of moderation, then so be it; otherwise, they annihilate them and erase their traces to preserve the well-being (صلاح) of the cosmos and maintain its stability (قوام).

Man, who is one of the parts of the cosmos, is not an exception to this universal principle (الكلية). If he proceeds according to what his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides him to, he attains the happiness (السعادة) predestined for him. But if he transgresses the limits of his nature and causes corruption in the land, Allah, the Glorified, seizes him with years of famine, exemplary punishments (المثلات), and various types of deterrents and vengeance, that perhaps he might return to righteousness and correctness. The Exalted said: ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of men have wrought, that He may make them taste something of what they have done, so that they may return) [30:41].

If they persist in corruption despite this, due to its rootedness in their souls, Allah seizes them with the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) and purifies the earth from the filth of their corruption. The Exalted said: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ (If the people of the towns had been faithful and Godwary, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn) [7:96]. And He said: وَما كانَ رَبُّكَ لِيُهْلِكَ الْقُرى بِظُلْمٍ وَأَهْلُها مُصْلِحُونَ (Your Lord would never destroy the towns unjustly while their people were righteous) [11:117]. And He said: أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ (My righteous servants shall inherit the earth) [21:105]. This is because if they are righteous, their deeds become righteous, and if their deeds are righteous, they agree with the general system, and through them, the earth becomes fit for their earthly life.

It has become clear from what has passed, firstly: that the reality of the prophetic call (دعوة النبوة) is the reformation (إصلاح) of earthly human life. The Exalted said, narrating from Shu‘ayb: إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ (I do not intend anything but reform so far as I can) [11:88].

Secondly: that His saying: وَلا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ (and do not obey the bid of the profligate, who cause corruption...) etc., despite the simplicity of its expression, relies upon a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية).

Perhaps in His saying: وَلا يُصْلِحُونَ (and do not set things right) after His saying: الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ (who cause corruption in the land) is an indication that it was expected of them — since they are human beings possessing a human primordial nature — to act righteously in the land, but they deviated from that nature and exchanged reformation for corruption.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مِنَ الْمُسَحَّرِينَ (They said, ‘You are only one of the bewitched) [26:153]. Meaning, one of those who has been bewitched (سحر) time after time until his intellect was overcome. It is also said that the suhr (السحر) is the upper part of the stomach, and the musahhar (المسحر) is one who has an interior (جوف), so it would be a metaphor for "you are a human being like us who eats and drinks." Thus, his saying after it: ما أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا (You are nothing but a human like us) would be a confirmation of it. It is also said that the musahhar is one who has a suhr, meaning a lung, as if they meant: "you are a breathing human like us."

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا (You are nothing but a human like us) until His saying: عَذابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (the punishment of a terrible day) [26:154-156]. The word shirb (الشرب) with a kasra on the shin means a share of water. The rest is clear, and the details of the story have already preceded in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: فَعَقَرُوها فَأَصْبَحُوا نادِمِينَ (But they hamstrung her, and then they became regretful) [26:157]. The attribution of the hamstringing (العقر) to the group — even though only one of them hamstrung her — is due to their satisfaction (رضا) with his act. In Nahj al-Balagha: "O people, indeed what brings people together is satisfaction and anger. Indeed, only one man hamstrung the she-camel of Thamud, but Allah encompassed them all with the punishment when they all encompassed it with satisfaction. Thus, the Glorified said: 'But they hamstrung her, and then they became regretful.'"

His saying: فأصبحوا نادمين (and then they became regretful) — perhaps their regret was only upon witnessing the appearance of the signs of the punishment, even though they had said to him after the hamstringing, out of defiance and mockery: يا صالِحُ ائْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ (O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are one of the apostles) [7:77].

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذَهُمُ الْعَذابُ (So the punishment seized them) until His saying: الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ (the All-mighty, the All-merciful) [26:158-159]. The definite article (اللام) is for reference (للعهد), meaning the promised punishment seized them, for Salih had promised them the descent of the punishment after three days, as in Surah Hud. The rest is clear.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٦٠)
The people of Lot impugned the apostles (160)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٦١)
when Lot, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (161)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٦٢)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (162)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٦٣)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (163)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٤)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (164)
أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلذُّكْرَانَ مِنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٥)
What! Of all people do you come to males, (165)
وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ (١٦٦)
abandoning your wives your Lord has created for you? Indeed, you are a transgressing lot.’ (166)
قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰلُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْرَجِينَ (١٦٧)
They said, ‘Lot, if you do not desist, you will surely be banished.’ (167)
قَالَ إِنِّى لِعَمَلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَالِينَ (١٦٨)
He said, ‘Indeed I detest your conduct.’ (168)
رَبِّ نَجِّنِى وَأَهْلِى مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦٩)
‘My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do.’ (169)
فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٧٠)
So We delivered him and all his family, (170)
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (١٧١)
except an old woman who remained behind. (171)
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (١٧٢)
Then We destroyed [all] the rest, (172)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (١٧٣)
and rained down upon them a rain [of stones]. Evil was the rain of those who were warned! (173)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٤)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (174)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٧٥)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (175)

(Commentary)

The verses refer to the story of the Prophet Lot (peace be upon him) (لوط عليه‌السلام), who came after Salih (peace be upon him) (صالح عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — up to His saying — رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "The people of Lot impugned the apostles... Lord of all the worlds." The explanation of this has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتَأْتُونَ الذُّكْرانَ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ "What! Do you come to males from among all the worlds?" (26:165). The interrogative is for denunciation (الإنكار) and rebuke (التوبيخ). The term "males" (الذكران) is the plural of "male" (ذكر), the opposite of female. "Coming to them" (إتيانهم) is a metaphor for sodomy (اللواط), which had become widespread among them. "All the worlds" (العالمين) is the plural of "world" (عالم), referring here to groups of people.

His saying: مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ "from among all the worlds" could be connected to the pronoun of the subject in تَأْتُونَ "you come," meaning: "Do you, from among all the worlds, commit this heinous act?" In this case, it would carry the meaning of His saying in another place: ما سَبَقَكُمْ بِها مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ "No one in the world has ever preceded you in this" (7:80, 29:28).

Alternatively, it could be connected to His saying: الذُّكْرانَ "the males." The meaning would then be: "Do you, from among all the worlds—despite their vast numbers and the inclusion of women—marry only the men?"

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَذَرُونَ ما خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ "and leave what your Lord has created for you of your wives?..." (26:166). The word تَذَرُونَ means "you leave" or "you abandon," and it has no past tense form derived from its own root.

One who contemplates the creation of man and the division of its individuals into the two categories of male and female—and the organs and instruments with which each category is equipped, as well as the specificities of their creation—will not doubt that the purpose of the Fashioner and Originator (الصنع والإيجاد) in this diverse formation, and in placing the instinct of desire (غريزة الشهوة) in both groups and differentiating their roles through action (الفعل) and reception (الانفعال), was to bring them together through marriage (النكاح). This serves as a means for procreation (التناسل), which preserves the survival of the species (بقاء النوع) until a destined time.

Thus, the human male, insofar as he is a male, is created for the female, not for a male like himself; and the human female, insofar as she is a female, is created for the male, not for a female like herself. What is specific to the male in his creation is for the female, and what is specific to the female in her creation is for the male. This is the natural pairing (الزوجية الطبيعية) which the Fashioner and Originator (الصنع والإيجاد) contracted between the human male and female, making them a pair (زوجين).

Subsequently, social or religious aims and ends established among people the custom of social, conventional marriage (النكاح الاجتماعي الاعتباري), which involves a type of exclusivity between the spouses and a form of regulation for the aforementioned natural pairing. Thus, the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the specific creation (الخلقة) guide man toward the pairing of men with women, not men with men, and the pairing of women with men, not women with women. Furthermore, marriage is built upon the principle of procreation and reproduction (التوالد والتناسل), rather than mere partnership in life in a general sense.

From this, it becomes clear that the most likely meaning of His saying: ما خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ "what your Lord has created for you" refers to the organ permitted for men from women through marriage. The preposition "for" (اللام) denotes natural possession (الملك الطبيعي). The word مِنْ "of" in His saying: مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ "of your wives" is for partitivity (التبعيض), and the "pairing" (الزوجية) here refers to natural pairing, although it is possible to interpret it as social, conventional pairing in a certain sense.

As for the suggestion by some that the relative pronoun ما "what" refers to the women themselves, and that His saying مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ "of your wives" is an explanation (بيان) for it, this is far-fetched.

His saying: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ عادُونَ "Rather you are a transgressing people" (26:166). That is, you are those who overstep and depart from the boundary delineated for you by the primordial nature (الفطرة) and creation (الخلقة). It carries the same meaning as His saying: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ "Do you indeed come to men and cut off the way?" (29:29).

It has become evident from all that has passed that the speech of Lot (peace be upon him) is built upon a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) that has been alluded to.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يا لُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُخْرَجِينَ "They said, ‘If you do not desist, O Lot, you will surely be among the expelled’" (26:167). That is, those who are distanced and banished from our town, as is narrated from them in another place: أَخْرِجُوا آلَ لُوطٍ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ "Expel the family of Lot from your town" (27:56).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنِّي لِعَمَلِكُمْ مِنَ الْقالِينَ "He said, ‘I am indeed one of those who detest your conduct’" (26:168). What is meant by "their conduct" (عملهم)—as indicated by the context—is their coming to males and abandoning females. The term القالي means the one who detests or hates. Meeting their threat of banishment with such a statement, without addressing the threat itself, conveys the meaning: "I do not fear being expelled from your town, nor do I care for it; rather, I detest your conduct and desire salvation from its evil consequence (وبال) which will inevitably descend upon you." For this reason, he followed it with his saying: رَبِّ نَجِّنِي وَأَهْلِي مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ "My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do" (26:169).

His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ نَجِّنِي وَأَهْلِي مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ "My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do." This means either from the essence of the act they perform within his sight and hearing—as he was repulsed by it—or from the evil consequence of their act and the punishment (العذاب) that would inevitably follow it.

He mentioned only himself and his family because not a single person from the inhabitants of the town had believed in him. The Exalted said regarding this: فَما وَجَدْنا فِيها غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ "but We did not find in it more than one house of Muslims" (51:36).

His saying, the Exalted: فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَأَهْلَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ — up to His saying — الْآخَرِينَ "So We delivered him and his family, all of them... the others" (26:170-172). The term "the one who remained behind" (الغابر) means, as has been said, the one who stays after those who were with them have departed. "Destruction" (التدمير) means annihilation (الإهلاك). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَراً "And We rained down upon them a rain..." (26:173). This refers to the baked clay (السجيل), as the Exalted said: وَأَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ "and We rained down upon them stones of baked clay" (15:74).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً — up to His saying — الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ "There is indeed a sign in that... the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (26:174-175). The explanation of this has preceded.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَ أَصْحَـٰبُ لْـَٔيْكَةِ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٧٦)
The inhabitants of Aykah impugned the apostles, (176)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٧٧)
when Shu‘ayb said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (177)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٧٨)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (178)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٧٩)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (179)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٨٠)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (180)
۞ أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْسِرِينَ (١٨١)
Observe the full measure, and do not be of those who give short measure. (181)
وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ (١٨٢)
Weigh with an even balance, (182)
وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (١٨٣)
and do not cheat the people of their goods. Do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption. (183)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلْجِبِلَّةَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٨٤)
Be wary of Him who created you and the earlier generations.’ (184)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (١٨٥)
They said, ‘Indeed you are one of the bewitched. (185)
وَمَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَإِن نَّظُنُّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (١٨٦)
You are just a human being like us, and we indeed consider you to be a liar. (186)
فَأَسْقِطْ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١٨٧)
Make a fragment of the sky falls upon us, should you be truthful.’ (187)
قَالَ رَبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨٨)
He said, ‘My Lord knows best what you are doing.’ (188)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمِ ٱلظُّلَّةِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٨٩)
So they impugned him, and then they were overtaken by the punishment of the day of the overshadowing cloud. It was indeed the punishment of a terrible day. (189)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٩٠)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (190)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٩١)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (191)

(Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him), who was one of the Arab prophets, and it is the last of the seven stories mentioned in this Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّبَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — The inhabitants of Aykah impugned the apostles — until His saying — رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — Lord of all the worlds. (26:176-180). The Aykah (الأيكة) is a thicket or a grove with dense, tangled trees. It is said that it was a wooded grove near Midian inhabited by a group of people to whom Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) was sent. He was not one of them by lineage, which is why it says: إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ — when Shu‘ayb said to them (26:177), and it does not say "their brother Shu‘ayb," unlike Hud and Salih, who were related by lineage to their respective peoples. Similarly, Lut was related to his people through marriage. Therefore, the Quran refers to them by saying: أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ — their brother Hud (26:124), أَخُوهُمْ صالِحٌ — their brother Salih (26:142), and أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ — their brother Lut (26:161).

The explanation of the remaining verses has already been provided.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَلا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُخْسِرِينَ وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ — Give full measure, and do not be of those who cause loss. And weigh with an even balance. (26:181-182). Measure (الكيل) refers to that by which goods are estimated in terms of volume, and "giving full measure" (إيفاؤه) means not to diminish that volume. The balance (القسطاس) is the scale by which things are estimated in terms of weight, and its "evenness" (استقامته) means to weigh with justice. These two verses command justice in taking and giving through measuring and weighing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — And do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption. (26:183). Defrauding (البخس) is the reduction in weight and estimation, just as causing loss (الإخسار) is the reduction in the principal capital.

The apparent context suggests that His saying: وَلا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ — And do not cheat the people of their goods — meaning their commodities and belongings—is a supplementary condition to His saying: وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ — And weigh with an even balance. Similarly, His saying: وَلا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُخْسِرِينَ — and do not be of those who cause loss — is a supplementary condition to His saying: أَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ — Give full measure. His saying: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption — is an emphasis of both prohibitions, namely: لا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُخْسِرِينَ — do not be of those who cause loss — and لا تَبْخَسُوا — do not cheat — and an explanation of the evil and ill-omened consequence of giving short measure (التطفيف).

Regarding His saying: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ — and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption — the terms al-‘athyu (العثي) and al-‘aythu (العيث) both mean to cause corruption. Thus, the word مُفْسِدِينَ — causing corruption — is an emphatic circumstantial qualifier (حال مؤكد). We have already discussed how giving short measure (التطفيف) corrupts human society in the story of Shu‘ayb in Surah Hud, and in the commentary on the verse: وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلاً — and weigh with an even balance. That is better and more favorable in outcome (17:35) in Surah al-Isra; so refer back to those sections.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ وَالْجِبِلَّةَ الْأَوَّلِينَ — And be wary of Him who created you and the former generations. (26:184). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The jibillah (الجبلة) is the nature (الخليقة) upon which a thing is fashioned." [End quote]. Thus, what is meant by al-jibillah is "those possessed of nature," meaning: be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) of the One who created you and your forefathers of the former generations, whom He originated and in whose primordial nature (جبلتهم) He instilled the loathing of corruption and the recognition of its ill-omened nature.

Perhaps this meaning we have alluded to is the reason for specifically mentioning the jibillah (الجبلة). In any case, the verse is a call to monotheism in worship (توحيد العبادة), for they were not being God-conscious (يتقون) of the Creator, who is the Lord of all the worlds.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مِنَ الْمُسَحَّرِينَ — They said, ‘You are just one of the bewitched’ — until His saying — وَإِنْ نَظُنُّكَ لَمِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ — and we indeed consider you to be a liar. (26:185-186). The explanation of the beginning has already passed, and the word in (إِنْ) in His saying إِنْ نَظُنُّكَ is the lightened form (مخففة) of the heavy inna (الثقيلة).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَسْقِطْ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ — So cause a fragment of the sky to fall on us — etc. (26:187). The word kisaf (كسفاً), with a kasrah then a fathah—according to what has been said—is the plural of kisfah (كسفة), which means a piece or fragment. Their command is based on challenging him and mockery.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ — He said, ‘My Lord knows best what you do.’ (26:188). This was Shu‘ayb’s response to their statement and their demand for the punishment to come. It is an allusion to the fact that he has no authority in the matter; rather, the matter belongs to Allah, for He knows best what they do and whether their actions warrant punishment, and what that punishment should be if it is warranted. It is similar to Hud’s statement to his people: إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ — He said, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I communicate to you what I have been sent with’ (46:23).

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ — So they impugned him, and the punishment of the day of the Cloud overtook them — etc. (26:189). The "Day of the Cloud" (يوم الظلة) was the day when the people of Shu‘ayb were punished by a canopy of clouds. The details of their story have already been mentioned in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً — There is indeed a sign in that — until His saying — الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ — the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (26:190-191). The explanation of this has already passed.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Jawami‘ al-Jami‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ — when Shu‘ayb said to them — it is mentioned in a hadith: أن شعيبا أخا مدين ـ أرسل إليهم وإلى أصحاب الأيكة — "That Shu‘ayb, the brother of Midian, was sent to them and to the inhabitants of the thicket (أصحاب الأيكة)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّقُوا الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ وَالْجِبِلَّةَ الْأَوَّلِينَ — And be wary of Him who created you and the former generations — he said: الخلق الأولين — "The former creations." Regarding His saying: فَكَذَّبُوهُ — So they impugned him — he said: "The people of Shu‘ayb." Regarding: فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ — and the punishment of the day of the Cloud overtook them — he said: يوم حر وسمائم — "A day of intense heat and scorching winds."

Ayahs

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَنزِيلُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٩٢)
This is indeed [a Book] sent down by the Lord of all the worlds, (192)
نَزَلَ بِهِ ٱلرُّوحُ ٱلْأَمِينُ (١٩٣)
brought down by the Trustworthy Spirit (193)
عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ (١٩٤)
upon your heart (so that you may be one of the warners), (194)
بِلِسَانٍ عَرَبِىٍّ مُّبِينٍ (١٩٥)
in a clear Arabic language. (195)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِى زُبُرِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٩٦)
It is indeed [foretold] in the scriptures of the ancients. (196)
أَوَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ ءَايَةً أَن يَعْلَمَهُۥ عُلَمَـٰٓؤُا۟ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٩٧)
Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel recognize it? (197)
وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضِ ٱلْأَعْجَمِينَ (١٩٨)
Had We sent it down upon some non-Arab (198)
فَقَرَأَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ (١٩٩)
and had he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it. (199)
كَذَٰلِكَ سَلَكْنَـٰهُ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٢٠٠)
This is how We let it pass through the hearts of the guilty: (200)
لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٢٠١)
they do not believe in it until they sight the painful punishment. (201)
فَيَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٠٢)
It will overtake them suddenly while they are unaware. (202)
فَيَقُولُوا۟ هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ (٢٠٣)
Thereupon they will say, ‘Shall we be granted any respite?’ (203)
أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٢٠٤)
So do they seek to hasten on Our punishment? (204)
أَفَرَءَيْتَ إِن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ سِنِينَ (٢٠٥)
Tell me, should We let them enjoy for some years, (205)
ثُمَّ جَآءَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (٢٠٦)
then there comes to them what they have been promised, (206)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُمَتَّعُونَ (٢٠٧)
of what avail to them will be that which they were given to enjoy? (207)
وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَهَا مُنذِرُونَ (٢٠٨)
We have not destroyed any town without its having warners, (208)
ذِكْرَىٰ وَمَا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٠٩)
for the sake of admonition, and We were not unjust. (209)
وَمَا تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ (٢١٠)
It has not been brought down by the devils. (210)
وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُمْ وَمَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٢١١)
Neither does it behoove them, nor are they capable [of doing that]. (211)
إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ (٢١٢)
Indeed, they are kept at bay [even] from hearing it. (212)
فَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُعَذَّبِينَ (٢١٣)
So do not invoke any god besides Allah, lest you should be among the punished. (213)
وَأَنذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ ٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ (٢١٤)
Warn the nearest of your kinsfolk, (214)
وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢١٥)
and lower your wing to the faithful who follow you. (215)
فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٢١٦)
But if they disobey you, say, ‘I am absolved of what you do.’ (216)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٢١٧)
And put your trust in the All-mighty, the All-merciful, (217)
ٱلَّذِى يَرَىٰكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (٢١٨)
who sees you when you stand [for prayer], (218)
وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِى ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٢١٩)
and your going about among those who prostrate. (219)
إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٢٢٠)
Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (220)
هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن تَنَزَّلُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ (٢٢١)
Should I inform you on whom the devils descend? (221)
تَنَزَّلُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ (٢٢٢)
They descend on every sinful liar. (222)
يُلْقُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كَـٰذِبُونَ (٢٢٣)
They eavesdrop, and most of them are liars. (223)
وَٱلشُّعَرَآءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ ٱلْغَاوُۥنَ (٢٢٤)
As for the poets, [only] the perverse follow them. (224)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ (٢٢٥)
Have you not regarded that they rove in every valley, (225)
وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ مَا لَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٢٢٦)
and that they say what they do not do? (226)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱنتَصَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَىَّ مُنقَلَبٍ يَنقَلِبُونَ (٢٢٧)
Barring those who have faith, do righteous deeds, and remember Allah much often, and vindicate themselves after they have been wronged. And the wrongdoers will soon know at what goal they will end up. (227)

(Commentary)

The verses point toward what serves as a conclusion derived from the seven preceding stories, encompassing a rebuke and a threat directed at the disbelievers (كفار) of the nation.

Within them is a defense of the prophethood (نبوة) of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by arguing for it through its mention in the scriptures of the ancients (زبر الأولين) and the knowledge of the scholars of the Children of Israel (علماء بني إسرائيل) regarding him. There is also a defense of his Book by arguing that it is neither from the promptings of the devils (الشياطين) nor from the utterances of poets (الشعراء).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَتَنْزِيلُ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — And indeed, it is a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds (192). The pronoun refers to the Quran. In this, there is a return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah in His saying: تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ — These are the signs of the Manifest Book (2). It also serves as a follow-up to the discussion of their disbelief in it, as in His subsequent saying: وَما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ ، فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا — There does not come to them any new reminder from the All-Beneficent but that they disregard it. They have already denied [it]... (5-6).

The terms "sending down" (التنزيل) and "descending" (الإنزال) carry the same meaning, except that the form if‘āl (إفعال) usually denotes a sudden, all-at-once occurrence, while the form taf‘īl (تفعيل) denotes a gradual process. The linguistic origin of "descent" (النزول) regarding physical bodies is the movement of a body from a high place to a lower one; regarding non-physical entities, it is used in a manner appropriate to them.

His "sending down" (تنزيله), the Exalted, is His bringing a thing forth from His presence to the realm of creation (الخلق) and determination (التقدير). He has described Himself as the All-Exalted (العلي), the All-Magnificent (العظيم), the All-Great (الكبير), the Most High (المتعال), the Exalter of Ranks (رفيع الدرجات), and the Subduer (القاهر) above His servants. Thus, the emergence of a thing by His bringing it into existence from His presence into the world of creation and determination—or if you prefer, His bringing it forth from the world of the Unseen (عالم الغيب) to the world of the Seen (عالم الشهادة)—is considered a "sending down" (تنزيل) from Him, the Exalted.

"Descending" (الإنزال) and "sending down" (التنزيل) have been used in His speech, the Exalted, regarding various things with this specific nuance, such as His saying: يا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمْ لِباساً يُوارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ — O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness (al-A'raf: 26); and His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ — ...and He sent down for you eight mates of the cattle (az-Zumar: 6); and His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ — ...and We sent down iron, wherein is great might (al-Hadid: 25); and His saying: ما يَوَدُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَلَا الْمُشْرِكِينَ أَنْ يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book nor the polytheists wish that any good should be sent down to you from your Lord (al-Baqarah: 105). He stated this generally in His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — There is not a thing but its treasures are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure (al-Hijr: 21).

Among the verses indicating this meaning specifically regarding the Quran is His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ — We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise (az-Zukhruf: 3-4).

The "sending down" (التنزيل) was attributed to the "Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين) to indicate the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — of the Lord, the Exalted, because it has been repeatedly mentioned that the polytheists (المشركون) only acknowledged Him as the Lord of lords, but did not view Him as the Lord of all the worlds.

His saying, the Exalted: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ — The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language (193-195). What is meant by the Trustworthy Spirit (الروح الأمين) is Gabriel (جبرئيل), the angel of revelation (ملك الوحي), based on the evidence of His saying: مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — [Say,] ‘Whoever is an enemy of Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down to your heart by Allah’s will’ (al-Baqarah: 97). He named him in another place the Holy Spirit (روح القدس): قُل نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِّ — Say, ‘The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord with the truth’ (an-Nahl: 102). Discussion regarding the meaning of the Spirit (الروح) has already preceded in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah an-Nahl and Surah al-Isra.

The Spirit was described as "Trustworthy" (الأمين) to indicate that he is secure in his mission from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; he does not change anything of the speech of the Exalted through substitution (تبديل) or distortion (تحريف), whether intentionally, by mistake, or through forgetfulness, just as his description in another verse as "Holy" (القدس) points to this.

His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ — the preposition bā’ (ب) is for transitivity (التعدية), meaning "the Trustworthy Spirit brought it down." As for the opinion of those who say the bā’ is for accompaniment (المصاحبة), meaning "the Spirit descended with it," it is not to be considered, because the emphasis in this context is on the descent of the Quran, not the descent of the Spirit alongside the Quran.

The pronoun in نَزَلَ بِهِ refers to the Quran as speech composed of words that possess true meanings. Indeed, the words of the Quran are descended from Him, the Exalted, just as its meanings are descended from Him, as is apparent from His saying: فَإِذا قَرَأْناهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ — So when We have recited it, follow its recitation (al-Qiyamah: 18), and His saying: تِلْكَ آياتُ اللهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِّ — These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you with the truth (Al ‘Imran: 108, al-Jathiyah: 6), among others.

Therefore, no weight is given to the claim of those who say that what the Trustworthy Spirit brought down were only the meanings of the Noble Quran, and then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله expressed them with words corresponding to and narrating them in an Arabic tongue.

Even more absurd is the claim of those who say that the Quran, in both its wording and meaning, is among the productions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, projected from a level of his noble soul (نفس) called the Trustworthy Spirit to another level of it called the heart (القلب).

What is meant by the "heart" (القلب) to which perception (الإدراك) and consciousness (الشعور) are attributed in His speech, the Exalted, is the human soul (النفس الإنسانية) which possesses perception and to which all types of consciousness and will (الإرادة) terminate, rather than the pineal-shaped flesh suspended on the left side of the chest, which is one of the primary organs. This is understood from various places in His speech, the Exalted, such as His saying: وَبَلَغَتِ الْقُلُوبُ الْحَناجِرَ — ...and the hearts reached the throats (al-Ahzab: 10), meaning the spirits (الأرواح); and His saying: فَإِنَّهُ آثِمٌ قَلْبُهُ — ...his heart is indeed sinful (al-Baqarah: 283), meaning his soul, as there is no meaning in attributing sin to a specific physical organ.

Perhaps the reason for His saying: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart — rather than saying "upon you" (عليك), is to point to the manner in which he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله received the Quran revealed to him; that what received it from the Spirit was his noble soul, without the participation of the external senses (الحواس الظاهرة) which are the tools used for perceiving particular matters (الأمور الجزئية).

Thus, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would see and hear while the revelation (الوحي) was being given to him without using the senses of sight and hearing, as it is narrated that he would be overtaken by a state resembling a faint, called the "heaviness of revelation" (برجاء الوحي).

He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would see the person and hear the voice just as we see a person and hear a voice, except that he did not use his material senses of sight and hearing for that as we use them.

If his seeing and hearing were through material sight and hearing, then what he experienced would have been shared between him and others; other people would have seen what he saw and heard what he heard. Definitive transmission (النقل القطعي) refutes this, for he was often overtaken by the state of revelation while among people, and revelation would be given to him while those around him perceived nothing, nor did they witness any person speaking to him or any speech being delivered to him.

The claim that it is possible for Allah, the Exalted, to divert the senses of others from some of what his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله senses were perceiving—namely the matters of the Unseen (الأمور الغيبية) hidden from us—is a destruction of the foundation of scientific affirmation (التصديق العلمي). For if such a grave error were possible regarding the senses, which are the key to necessary knowledge (العلوم الضرورية) and self-evident affirmations (التصديقات البديهية) and others, no trust would remain in any knowledge or affirmation.

Furthermore, this argument is based on the primacy of the senses (أصالة الحس) and the notion that nothing exists except the sensible (المحسوس), which is a most egregious error. Discussion regarding the meaning of the angel's manifestation (تمثل الملك) has already preceded in the commentary of Surah Maryam, which is relevant here.

It has been said that the reason for specifying the heart for the descent is that it is the perceiving and legally responsible (المكلف) entity rather than the body, even if it receives the revelation through the mediation of bodily tools like hearing and sight; you have already seen the flaw in this.

It has also been said: Since the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had two aspects—an angelic aspect (جهة ملكية) through which he receives emanation, and a human aspect (جهة بشرية) through which he provides emanation—the descent was placed upon his spirit because it is what is characterized by the angelic attributes through which he receives from the Trustworthy Spirit. To point to this, it was said: عَلى قَلْبِكَ (to your heart) and it did not say "upon you," despite the latter being more concise. [End of quote].

This too is based on the participation of the senses and bodily faculties in receiving revelation, so the same refutation we provided earlier applies to it.

A group of commentators (المفسرين) mentioned that what is meant by the heart is the specific physical organ, and that perception, in whatever form, is one of its properties.

Among them are those who said: Making the heart the object of the descent is based on figurative extension (التوسع), because Allah, the Exalted, makes Gabriel hear the Quran by creating the sound, so he memorizes it and descends with it to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and recites it to him, so he comprehends it and preserves it in his heart; thus, it is as if it descended upon his heart.

Among them are those who said: The specification of the heart for the descent is because spiritual meanings (المعاني الروحانية) descend first upon the spirit, then transfer from it to the heart due to the connection between them, then transfer from it to the brain, where they are inscribed upon the tablet of the imagination (المتخيلة).

Among them are those who said: Its specification is to point to the perfection of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله intellection (تعقله), as he did not require intermediaries like hearing, sight, and others.

Among them are those who said: This is to point to the righteousness and sanctity of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله heart, as it was the dwelling place for the speech of the Exalted, so that the righteousness of the rest of his parts and limbs might be known through it; for the heart is the chief of all limbs and their king, and if the king is righteous, the subjects are righteous.

Among them are those who said: This is because Allah, the Exalted, granted the heart of His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله a specific hearing and sight through which he hears and sees, distinguishing his status from others, as suggested by His saying, the Exalted: ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى — The heart did not feign what it saw (an-Najm: 11).

These views, in addition to most of them containing conjecture, are based on analogizing these matters of the Unseen to the material events we have and applying their rules to them. The arbitrariness of some reached the point of saying: The meaning of the angel bringing down the Quran is that Allah inspired him with His speech while he was in heaven and taught him its recitation, then the angel delivered it on earth while descending in space. In this, there are two methods: one is that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله divested himself of the human form into the angelic form and took it from the angel; the second is that the angel divested himself into the human form so that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله could take it from him, and the first is the more difficult of the two states. [End of quote].

I wonder what he imagined regarding a human divesting himself of his form into an angelic form and becoming an angel, then returning to being a human, and an angel divesting himself into a human form, when he has assumed for each of them an identity (هوية) distinct from the other, with no link between them in essence (ذاتا) or effect (أثرا). There are other points in his speech for consideration that are not hidden from one who reflects upon it.

There is a completion to this research; perhaps Allah, the Glorified, will grant us the success to fulfill it by providing a comprehensive discourse on the angel (الملك) and another on revelation (الوحي).

His saying: لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ — ...so that you may be one of the warners (194); meaning those who call toward Allah, the Glorified, by frightening people with His punishment. This is what is meant by "warning" (الإنذار) in the parlance of the Quran, rather than the Prophet or Messenger specifically. The Exalted said regarding the believers among the Jinn: وَإِذْ صَرَفْنا إِلَيْكَ نَفَراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقُرْآنَ فَلَمَّا حَضَرُوهُ قالُوا أَنْصِتُوا فَلَمَّا قُضِيَ وَلَّوْا إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ مُنْذِرِينَ — When We stepped a group of jinn toward you to listen to the Quran, when they attended it, they said, ‘Listen!’ When it was finished, they returned to their people as warners (al-Ahqaf: 29). And He said regarding those who seek understanding among the believers: لِيَتَفَقَّهُوا فِي الدِّينِ وَلِيُنْذِرُوا قَوْمَهُمْ إِذا رَجَعُوا إِلَيْهِمْ — ...so that they may acquire a deep understanding of religion, and warn their people when they return to them (at-Tawbah: 122).

His صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله warning was mentioned as the goal of the Quran's descent, rather than his prophethood or messengership, because the context of the Surah's verses is one of frightening and threatening.

His saying: بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ — ...in a clear Arabic language (195); meaning manifest in its Arabicity or clarifying the objectives with complete clarity. The prepositional phrase is connected to "brought down" (نزل), meaning: He sent it down in a clear Arabic language.

Some have allowed it to be connected to His saying: الْمُنْذِرِينَ (the warners), meaning: He sent it down to your heart so that you may enter the group of prophets from the Arabs; among those mentioned in the Quran are Hud, Salih, Ishmael, and Shu'ayb (peace be upon them). The first of the two views is the better one.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُ لَفِي زُبُرِ الْأَوَّلِينَ — And indeed it is in the scriptures of the ancients (196). The pronoun refers to the Quran or its descent upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The term زبر (scriptures) is the plural of zabūr, which is a book. The meaning is: Indeed, the news of the Quran or the news of its descent upon you is in the books of the past prophets.

It has been said: The pronoun refers to the universal gnoses (المعارف الكلية) within the Quran; meaning that the Quranic gnoses are present and mentioned in the books of the past prophets.

Against this: Firstly, the polytheists did not believe in the prophets and their books such that they could be argued against by what is in them regarding monotheism, the Resurrection (المعاد), and others. This is unlike the mention of the news of the Quran and its descent upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in the books of the ancients, for it would then be a prophecy (ملحمة) that souls are compelled to accept.

Secondly, it does not harmonize with the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ آيَةً أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ عُلَماءُ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ — Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel know it? (197). The pronoun in أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ (that they know it) refers to the news of the Quran or the news of its descent upon the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. That is: Was not the knowledge of the scholars of the Children of Israel regarding the news of the Quran or its descent upon you—by way of glad tidings in the books of the past prophets—a sign for the polytheists of the truth of your prophethood? The Jews used to give glad tidings of this and seek victory over the Arabs through it, as mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: وَكانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — ...though before that they used to pray for victory over the faithless (al-Baqarah: 89).

A number of Jewish scholars embraced Islam during the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and admitted that he was prophesied in their books. This Surah is among the early Meccan Surahs revealed before the Migration (الهجرة), and the enmity of the Jews toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله had not reached the level it did after the Migration; it was hoped that they would speak some of the truth they possessed, even in a general way.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ فَقَرَأَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ — Had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs, and had he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it (198-199). It is said in al-Mufradat: "Non-Arabicity (العجمة) is the opposite of clarity (الإبانة), and i‘jām is ambiguity (الإبهام)..." until he said: "The ‘Ajam (العجم) are the opposite of the Arabs, and the ‘Ajamī is attributed to them. The A‘jam (الأعجم) is one in whose tongue there is non-Arabicity, whether he is an Arab or otherwise, considering their lack of understanding from the non-Arabs; from this, the beast is called ‘ajmā’. The A‘jamī is attributed to him. His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ — is with the deletion of the yā’s." [End of quote].

What he mentioned implies—as you see—that the origin of al-A‘jamīn is al-A‘jamiyyīn, then the yā’ of attribution was deleted; others have stated this explicitly. Some mentioned that the reason is that a‘jam has the feminine ‘ajmā’, and the form af‘al fa‘lā’ does not take the sound plural, but the Kufan grammarians allow this. The apparent wording supports their view, so there is no reason to claim a deletion.

In any case, the apparent context connects these two verses to His saying: بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ (in a clear Arabic language). Thus, they are in the position of providing the rationale for it. The meaning would be: We sent it down to you in an Arabic tongue of manifest Arabicity and clear indication so that they might believe in it and not make excuses based on their lack of understanding of its objectives. Had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs in a non-Arabic tongue, they would not have believed in it and would have rejected it due to not understanding its objectives.

Thus, the meaning of its descent upon some of the non-Arabs would be its descent in a non-Arabic language and in his tongue. These two verses and the one following them carry the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً أَعْجَمِيًّا لَقالُوا لَوْ لا فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُءَ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَعَرَبِيٌّ قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هُدىً وَشِفاءٌ وَالَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى — Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would have said, ‘Why are its signs not articulated? [What!
