A] non-Arabic [revelation] and an Arab [messenger]?’ Say, ‘It is guidance and a cure for those who have faith, and as for those who do not believe, there is a deafness in their ears and it is a blindness for them’ (Fussilat: 44).

Some have said: The meaning is that had We sent it down as an Arabic Quran as it is, with its exquisite and miraculous composition, to some of the non-Arabs who are unable to speak Arabic, and he recited it to them with a correct, miraculous recitation, they would not have believed in it—despite the joining of the miracle of recitation to the miracle of what is recited—due to the extremity of their stubbornness and the intensity of their persistence in obstinacy.

He said: As for the saying of some that the meaning is "had We sent it down to some of the non-Arabs in the language of the non-Arabs and he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it," it is not quite right, for it is far from being suitable for the context of explaining their persistence in obstinacy and stubbornness. [End of summary].

Against this: The connection of the two verses to His saying: بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ is closer to them than their connection to the context of the disbelievers' persistence in their disbelief and denial; you have already seen the clarification of this.

It could be objected to the previous view that the pronoun in His saying: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ refers to this Quran which is Arabic. So if the meaning were its descent in a non-Arabic tongue, the meaning would be: "Had We sent down the Arabic as non-Arabic," which is nonsensical.

This is refuted by the fact that it is like His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ — We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason (az-Zukhruf: 3). There is no meaning in our saying: "We made the Arabic Arabic." Thus, the "Quran" in any case refers to the recited Book.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ سَلَكْناهُ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ — Thus do We let it pass through the hearts of the guilty (200). The indication in His saying كَذلِكَ (thus) is to the state the Quran is in with the polytheists, which was mentioned in the preceding verses: that they disregard it and do not believe in it, even though it is a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds, is in clear Arabic and not non-Arabic, and was mentioned in the scriptures of the ancients and known by the scholars of the Children of Israel.

السلوك (passing through) means entering into a path and causing to pass. What is meant by "the guilty" (المجرمين) are the disbelievers and polytheists. They were mentioned with the attribute of guilt (الإجرام) to point to the cause of the judgment—which is its passing through their hearts in this hated and repelled state—and that this is a divine recompense with which He repaid them for their guilt, and so that the judgment may be general due to the generality of the cause.

The meaning is: In this state—which is such that it is disregarded and not believed in—We enter the Quran into the hearts of these polytheists and cause it to pass through their souls as a recompense for their guilt; and likewise for every guilty person.

It has been said: The indication is to the noble attributes of the Quran mentioned. The meaning would be: We enter the Quran and cause it to pass through the hearts of the guilty just as We have explained its attributes, so they see that it is a heavenly book with a miraculous composition beyond human power, and that it was prophesied in the scriptures of the ancients and known by the scholars of the Children of Israel, and the proof (الحجة) is completed against them through it. This is far from the context.

It has also been said: The pronoun in سَلَكْناهُ refers to the denial of the Quran and disbelief in it, as indicated by His saying: ما كانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ (they would not have believed in it). This is close to the first view, but the first view is more elegant and precise, and it was mentioned in al-Kashshaf.

It has become clear from what preceded that what is meant by "the guilty" are the polytheists of Mecca, except that the generality of the attribute of guilt generalizes the judgment. Some said: What is meant by "the guilty" are those other than the polytheists of Mecca among their contemporaries and those who come after them. The meaning would be: Just as We let it pass through the hearts of the polytheists of Mecca, We let it pass through the hearts of other guilty ones.

Perhaps what led him to choose this view was the problem of the identity of the thing likened (المشبه) and the thing likened to (المشبه به) in the first view, while there must be a distinction between them. So he considered the referent of كَذلِكَ to be the passing through the hearts of the polytheists of Mecca—which is the thing likened to—and made the thing likened to be other guilty ones. Against this: Likening a universal (الكلي) to some of its particulars (أفراده) to indicate the pervasiveness of its judgment in all particulars is a common method.

From here it appears that there is another view: that what is meant by "the guilty" is what encompasses the polytheists of Mecca and others, by making the definite article al- (ال) for other than specific reference (العهد). Perhaps the first view is closer to the context.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ — They will not believe in it until they sight the painful punishment (201) — until His saying — مُنْظَرُونَ (given any respite). This is an explanation and clarification of His saying: كَذلِكَ سَلَكْناهُ etc. This is according to the first and third views mentioned regarding the previous verse; as for the second view, it is a new sentence (استئناف) unrelated to what precedes it.

His saying: حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ — meaning until they witness the painful punishment, which will then force them into a compelled faith (الإيمان الاضطراري) that will not benefit them. It is apparent that what is meant by the painful punishment is what they witness at the time of death. Some considered it possible that what is meant is what befell them on the day of Badr of being killed, but the generality of the judgment in the previous verse for the polytheists of Mecca and others does not harmonize with that.

His saying: فَيَأْتِيَهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ — ...so that it will come upon them suddenly while they are unaware (202) — is like an explanation for His saying: حَتَّى يَرَوُا الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ. For if it did not come upon them suddenly and they knew of it before its appointed time, they would have prepared for it and believed by their own choice without being compelled.

His saying: فَيَقُولُوا هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنْظَرُونَ — ...and they will say, ‘Shall we be given any respite?’ (203) — is a word of regret from them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَبِعَذابِنا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ — Do they seek to hasten Our punishment? (204) — is a rebuke and a threat.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ مَتَّعْناهُمْ سِنِينَ — Tell Me, if We let them enjoy for some years (205) — until His saying — يُمَتَّعُونَ (they were given to enjoy). This is connected to His saying: فَيَقُولُوا هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنْظَرُونَ. The sum of the meaning is that the wish for delay and respite is a wish for something that would not benefit them if it occurred as they wished, and would not avail them anything if they were granted what they asked. For their being given enjoyment for a limited period, whether long or short, does not remove the eternal punishment that has been decreed against them.

This is His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ مَتَّعْناهُمْ سِنِينَ — a numbered few that will end; ثُمَّ جاءَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ — ...then there comes to them what they were promised (206) — of punishment after the passing of the years of respite and delay; ما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يُمَتَّعُونَ — ...what they were given to enjoy will not avail them (207) — meaning their enjoyment for a limited period.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَها مُنْذِرُونَ ذِكْرى — We have not destroyed any town but it had warners [to provide it] with a reminder (208-209) etc. It is most likely that His saying لَها مُنْذِرُونَ is a circumstantial clause (حال) for "town" (قرية), and His saying ذِكْرى (a reminder) is a circumstantial clause for the plural pronoun in "warners" (مُنْذِرُونَ), or an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) whose governing verb is "warners" because it carries the meaning of "reminders." The meaning is manifest. Other things have been said which are of no use to mention or prolong the discussion over.

His saying: وَما كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ — ...and We were not unjust (209). The entry of the negation upon the state of being (الكون) rather than saying "and We did not wrong them" or the like, expresses the negation of the very nature of the act (نفي الشأنية); meaning: it was not of Our nature, nor to be expected of Us, that We should wrong them.

The sentence is in the position of providing the rationale for the preceding restriction. The meaning is: We did not destroy any town except in a state where it had warners and reminders through whom the proof was completed against them; for if We had destroyed them in other than this state, We would have been unjust to them, and it is not of Our nature to wrong anyone. Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولاً — ...and We do not punish until We have sent an apostle (al-Isra: 15).

A Discourse on the Meaning of the Negation of Injustice (الظلم) from Him, the Exalted

Among the necessary concomitants of the meaning of injustice (الظلم) — which are equivalent to it — is the agent's act and his disposal of that which he does not own in terms of action and disposal. Its opposite is justice (العدل), and its concomitant is that it is the agent's act and his disposal of that which he owns.

From here it appears that the acts of ontological agents (الفواعل التكوينية), insofar as they are owned by them ontologically (تكونيا), do not manifest the meaning of injustice. This is because the assumption of an act's issuance from its agent ontologically is synonymous with its being owned by him, in the sense that its existence subsists through him in a manner that it has no independence without him.

For Allah, the Glorified, there is absolute ownership (ملك مطلق) extended over all things from all aspects of their existence, because they subsist through Him, the Exalted, without any self-sufficiency from Him or independence without Him. Thus, any disposal He makes in them—whether it pleases them or grieves them, benefits them or harms them—is not in any way part of injustice. Or if you prefer, say: it is justice in the sense of what is not injustice. He has the right to do what He wills and to judge as He intends, all of that according to the ontological order (التكوين).

Thus, He, the Exalted, possesses absolute ownership by His essence (بذاته), and other ontological agents possess an ontological ownership relative to their acts according to the divine bestowal and gift. This is an ownership in the longitudinal extension (في طول) of His ownership, the Exalted; He is the Owner of what He has made them own, and the Controller over that which He has given them authority.

Among these agents is the human species in relation to its acts, especially those we call voluntary acts (الأفعال الاختيارية) and the choice (الاختيار) by which these acts are determined. Each one of us finds within himself through direct witnessing that he owns choice, in the sense of the possibility of both acting and refraining. If he wills, he acts; and if he does not will, he refrains. He sees himself as free, owning the act and the refraining—whatever act or refraining they may be—in the sense of the possibility of either issuing from him.

Furthermore, the human necessity for social, civil life compelled the intellect (العقل) to overlook some of the freedom of action that the human possesses, and to withdraw the hand from some acts that he saw himself as owning—namely those whose performance would disrupt the order of society, thereby disrupting the order of his own life. These are the prohibitions (المحرمات) and sins (المعاصي) that are forbidden by civil laws, national customs, or the royal decrees prevalent in societies.

It is necessary for the enforcement of these laws and customs that some type of negative recompense (الجزاء السيئ) be established for those who violate them—on the condition of knowledge and the completion of the proof, as it is a condition for the realization of legal obligation (التكليف)—such as blame or punishment; and a type of beautiful reward (الأجر الجميل) for the obedient who respects them, such as praise or reward.

It is also necessary that there be someone appointed over the society and the laws current within it to enforce them as they are, and he is responsible for what he was appointed for, especially regarding the laws of recompense. For if he were not responsible, and it were permissible for him to recompense or not to recompense, and to take the doer of good while leaving the doer of evil, the establishment of laws and customs would be futile from the start. These are rational principles (أصول عقلائية) current generally in human societies since this species settled on the earth, originating from their human primordial nature (الفطرة).

Rational proofs have indicated, and the successive reports (تواتر) of the prophets and messengers from Him, the Exalted, have supported, that social laws and the customs of life must be from Him, the Exalted. These are human judgments and duties to which the human primordial nature (الفطرة الإنسانية) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides, and which guarantee the happiness of his life and preserve the interests of his society.

This heavenly, primordial law (الشريعة السماوية الفطرية), its Legislator is Allah, the Glorified, and its Enforcer in terms of reward and punishment—and their place is the place of return to Him, the Exalted—is Allah, the Glorified.

The requirement of His legislating these heavenly laws and His considering Himself their Enforcer is that He has made it obligatory upon Himself by a legislative obligation (إيجابا تشريعيا)—not an ontological one—that He shall not contradict Himself, nor fail by neglecting or canceling a recompense necessitated by a violation, or implementing a recompense not deserved by an act, such as punishing the heedless and ignorant with the punishment of the intentional and stubborn, or taking the wronged for the sin of the wrongdoer. Otherwise, it would be injustice (ظلم) from Him; Exalted is He far above that.

Perhaps this is the meaning of what is said: that injustice is within His power, the Exalted, but it never occurs, because it is a deficiency in perfection from which He, the Exalted, is transcendent. Thus, the assumption of injustice from Him, the Exalted, is the assumption of the impossible (المحال), but it is not an impossible assumption (ليس بفرض محال). This is what is understood from the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَما كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ (and We were not unjust), and His saying in verse 209 of the Surah: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً — Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least (Yunus: 44), and His saying: وَما رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ — ...and your Lord is not at all unjust to [His] servants (Fussilat: 46), and His saying: لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ — ...so that the people may not have any argument against Allah after the apostles (an-Nisa: 165). Their apparent meaning is that they are not of the type of a negative proposition due to the non-existence of the subject (السالبة بانتفاء الموضوع), as suggested by the commentary of those who explained them as meaning that Allah does not perform an act which, if performed by someone else, would be injustice.

If you say: What was mentioned regarding the necessity of enforcing recompense as reward or punishment contradicts what is accepted among them: that leaving the punishment of the sinner is permissible because it is the right of the punisher, and it is permissible for the possessor of a right to leave it and not demand it; unlike the reward of the obedient, because it is the right of another—namely the obedient—so it is not permissible to leave it or invalidate it.

Furthermore, it has been said: Rewarding for acts of obedience is from grace (الفضل) rather than entitlement (الاستحقاق), because the servant and his work belong to his Master, so he owns nothing such that he could exchange it for something.

I say: Leaving the punishment of the sinner in some cases (في الجملة) is not disputed, because it is from grace. But as for doing so entirely (بالجملة), then no, because it would necessitate the futility of legislation, law-making, and the arrangement of recompense upon action.

As for the reward of deeds being from grace—considering that the servant's work, like himself, belongs to Allah—this does not contradict another grace from Him, the Exalted, toward His servant by considering his work as his own property, then making what He rewards him with an "upholding" (أجر) for his work. The Quran is full of the mention of the reward (الأجر) for righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), and He, the Exalted, has said: إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَأَمْوالَهُمْ بِأَنَّ لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةَ — Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful their souls and their possessions for paradise to be theirs (at-Tawbah: 111).

His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّياطِينُ — The devils have not brought it down (210) — until His saying — لَمَعْزُولُونَ (are indeed barred). This is the beginning of the response to the polytheists' claim that Muhammad has a jinn who brings him this speech, and their claim that he is a poet. The response to the first was presented first. The speech was first directed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, explaining to him that the Quran is not from the descent of the devils, thereby comforting his soul; then the speech was directed to the people, explaining it to them in a way they could comprehend.

So His saying: وَما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّياطِينُ — meaning they did not bring it down. The verse is connected to His saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَتَنْزِيلُ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (And indeed, it is a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds). The speech is directed, as you heard, to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as evidenced by His saying immediately after: فَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ (So do not invoke any other god besides Allah) until the end of the addresses specific to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, which branch off from His saying: وَما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ etc., as will be explained.

The speech was directed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله rather than the people because it is reasoned with what they do not accept due to their disbelief—namely His saying: إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ (they are indeed barred from hearing). The "devil" (الشيطان) is the evil one, and its plural is "devils" (الشياطين), and what is meant by them are the evil ones among the jinn.

His saying: وَما يَنْبَغِي لَهُمْ — It does not behoove them (211); meaning the devils. It is said in al-Majma‘: "The meaning of the Arabs' saying 'it behooves you (ينبغي لك) to do such and such' is that you are requested to do it by the requirement of the intellect, from al-bughyah which is the request." [End of quote].

The reason why it does not behoove them to bring it down is that they are an evil creation whose only concern is evil, corruption, taking up falsehood, and depicting it in the form of truth to lead astray from the path of Allah. The Quran is the speech of truth (كلام حق) to which falsehood has no way; thus, it does not suit their diabolical nature to deliver it to anyone.

His saying: وَما يَسْتَطِيعُونَ — ...nor are they able to (211); meaning they are not capable of bringing it down because it is heavenly speech received by the angels from the Lord of Might, and they bring it down by His command under preservation and guardianship from Him, the Exalted, as He said: فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ — ...He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them (al-Jinn: 27-28). To this, His saying points: إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ etc.

His saying: إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ — Indeed they are barred from hearing [it] (212); meaning that the devils are barred from hearing heavenly news and becoming aware of what occurs in the Highest Assembly (الملإ الأعلى), as they are pelted with piercing flames (الشهب الثاقبة) if they try to listen, as Allah mentioned in various places of His speech.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُعَذَّبِينَ — So do not invoke any other god besides Allah, lest you should be among the punished (213). An address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله forbidding him from polytheism (الشرك) with Allah, branching off from His saying: وَما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّياطِينُ etc. That is: If this Quran is a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds and the devils did not bring it down, and it forbids polytheism and promises punishment for it, then do not associate anything with Allah, lest the promised punishment reach you and you enter the group of the punished.

The fact that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is protected by a divine infallibility (عصمة) — the divinely granted protection from sin and error — with which the issuance of sin from him is impossible, does not contradict his being forbidden from polytheism. For infallibility does not necessitate the invalidation of the command and prohibition's connection to the infallible, nor the removal of legal obligation from him insofar as he is a human possessing choice in action and refraining, for whom obedience and disobedience are conceivable in relation to himself. Verses regarding the legal obligation of the prophets (عليه‌السلام) are numerous in the Noble Quran, such as His saying regarding the prophets (عليه‌السلام): وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — ...and had they ascribed any partners [to Him], what they used to do would have come to naught (al-An'am: 88), and His saying to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ — ...if you ascribe a partner [to Allah], your deeds will surely come to naught (az-Zumar: 65). Both verses carry the meaning of prohibition.

The saying of some—that legal obligation is for the sake of completion (التكميل), so it is removed upon the attainment and realization of perfection because it is impossible to seek what is already attained—is an error. For the righteous deeds to which legal obligations are connected are among the effects of the sought perfection. Just as spiritual perfection (الكمال النفساني) must be acquired by performing its effects, practicing the deeds that suit it, and training through them, it must likewise be maintained through them. As long as the human is a human with a connection to earthly life, he has no escape from bearing the burdens of legal obligation. Discussion regarding this meaning has already preceded in some of the researches.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ — And warn your nearest relations (214). In al-Majma‘: "A man's ‘ashīrah is his kin; they were named so because he associates with them and they associate with him (ya‘āshiruhum)." [End of quote]. He specifically mentioned his kin and nearest relations after forbidding himself from polytheism and warning him, to draw attention to the fact that there is no exception in the religious call, nor any compromise or leniency, as is customary in royal traditions. There is no difference in the connection of the warning between the Prophet and his nation, nor between relatives and strangers; all are servants and Allah is their Master.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ...and lower your wing to the faithful who follow you (215); meaning occupy yourself with those who believe in you, gather them, and join them to yourself with kindness and mercy, just as a bird gathers its chicks to itself by lowering its wing to them. This is from the metaphor by metonymy (الاستعارة بالكناية); its like has preceded in His saying: وَاخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — ...and lower your wing to the faithful (al-Hijr: 88).

What is meant by "following" (الاتباع) is obedience, by the evidence of His saying in the following verse: فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — But if they disobey you, say, ‘I am indeed absolved of what you do’ (216). The summary of the meaning of the two verses is: If they believe in you and follow you, then gather them to yourself with kindness and occupy yourself with their upbringing; and if they disobey you, then disassociate yourself from their action.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ — And put your trust in the All-mighty, the All-merciful (217); meaning you have nothing of the matter of their obedience or disobedience beyond what We have obligated you with. So leave everything beyond that to Allah, the Glorified; for by His might (عزته) He will punish the disobedient, and by His mercy (رحمته) He will save the following believers.

In the specific choice of the names the All-mighty (العزيز) and the All-merciful (الرحيم), there is a drawing of the mind to the stories that preceded, each of which was concluded one after another with these two noble names.

It is in the sense of saying: Put your trust in the matter of the followers and the disobedient alike in Allah; for He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful, who did to the people of Noah, Hud, Salih, Abraham, Lot, Shu'ayb, and the people of Pharaoh what He did of what We have narrated. His sunnah (سنة) — the established and unalterable way of Allah's action in creation — is the seizing of the disobedient and the saving of the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي يَراكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِي السَّاجِدِينَ — ...who sees you when you stand [for prayer], and your transitions among those who prostrate (218-219). The apparent meaning of the two verses—according to what first comes to mind—is that what is meant by "those who prostrate" (الساجدين) are those who prostrate in prayer among the believers, among whom was the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in his leading them in congregational prayer. What is meant, by the evidence of the contrast, is standing in prayer. The meaning would be: Who sees you while you are under His eye in the two states of your standing and your prostrating, transitioning among those who prostrate while you are praying with the believers.

Regarding the meaning of the verse, there are narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis which we will address in the coming traditional research (البحث الروائي), Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ — Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing (220). A rationale for His saying: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ.

In the verses—according to what has preceded of their meaning—is a consolation for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, glad tidings for the believers of salvation, and a threat to the disbelievers of punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلى مَنْ تَنَزَّلُ الشَّياطِينُ — Should I inform you on whom the devils descend? (221) — until His saying — كاذِبُونَ (are liars). A definition of those upon whom the devils descend by the attribute that characterizes them, so that it may be known that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is not among them, nor is the Quran from the promptings of the devils. The address is directed to the polytheists.

So His saying: هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلى مَنْ تَنَزَّلُ الشَّياطِينُ — is in the sense of: Shall I make known to you those upon whom the devils of the jinn descend with news?

His saying: تَنَزَّلُ عَلى كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ — They descend on every lying sinner (222). It is said in al-Majma‘: "The affāk is the liar; the origin of ifk is turning, and the affāk is one who frequently turns the news from the side of truth to the side of falsehood. The athīm is the doer of the base; it is said athima ya’thamu ithman when he commits the base, and ta’aththama when he leaves the sin." [End of quote].

This is because the devils have no concern except manifesting falsehood in the form of truth and beautifying the base in the dress of the good; thus, they do not descend except upon a lying sinner.

His saying: يُلْقُونَ السَّمْعَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كاذِبُونَ — They impart what they have heard, and most of them are liars (223). It is apparent that the two plural pronouns in يُلْقُونَ (they impart) and أَكْثَرُهُمْ (most of them) both refer to the devils. "Hearing" (السمع) is a verbal noun meaning "that which is heard," and what is meant by it is what the devils heard of the news of heaven, even if incomplete; for they are prevented from listening and pelted with flames, so what they snatch away cannot be anything but incomplete, neither finished nor perfect, and therefore falsehood frequently seeps into it.

His saying: وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كاذِبُونَ — meaning most of the devils are liars who do not report truth at all; this is the "most" in terms of individuals. It is also possible that what is meant is "most" in terms of the descent; meaning most of those among them who descend are liars, i.e., most of their reports are false.

The summary of the argument of the three verses is that the devils, because their nature is built upon evil, do not descend except upon every lying profligate, and most of them are liars in their reports. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is not a lying sinner, nor is the speech revealed to him a fabricated lie. Thus, he is not among those upon whom the devils descend, nor is the one who descends upon him a devil, nor is the Quran revealed to him from the promptings of the devils.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ — As for the poets, [only] the erring follow them (224) — until His saying — لا يَفْعَلُونَ (they do not do). A response to the polytheists' charging the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with being a poet. He drew attention to it after the response to their saying that he has a devil who reveals the Quran to him.

These two—namely their saying that there is one of the jinn who comes to him, and their saying that he is a poet—were among what they used to repeat on their tongues in Mecca before the Migration to repel the true call. This is among what supports the descent of these verses in Mecca, contrary to what has been said that they were revealed in Medina.

Furthermore, the verses contain the conclusion of the Surah, namely His saying: وَسَيَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَيَّ مُنْقَلَبٍ يَنْقَلِبُونَ (And the wrongdoers will soon know at what goal they will end up). There is no meaning in a Surah that is among the oldest Meccan Surahs remaining for years in a state of incompleteness, then being completed in Medina. Nor is there any indication in the exception that the excepted ones are the poets of the believers after the Migration.

In any case, error (الغي) is the opposite of right guidance (الرشد), which is hitting upon the reality. The rightly guided (الرشيد) is he who only concerns himself with what is true and real, and the erring (الغوي) is he who walks the path of falsehood and misses the way of truth. Error and wandering (الغواية) are among what characterizes the craft of poetry, which is built upon imagination (التخييل) and depicting the non-real in the form of the real. Therefore, only the erring person, infatuated with imaginative decorations and illusory depictions that distract from the truth and divert from right guidance, concerns himself with it. None follow the poets whose craft is built upon error and wandering except the erring; and that is His saying, the Exalted: وَالشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ.

His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي كُلِّ وادٍ يَهِيمُونَ وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ ما لا يَفْعَلُونَ — Do you not see that they roam in every valley, and that they say what they do not do? (225-226). It is said hāma yahīmu hayamānan when one wanders aimlessly. What is meant by their roaming in every valley is their lack of restraint in speech without stopping at any limit; they might praise the censured falsehood just as the praised truth is praised, and they might lampoon the beautiful just as the ugly and base is lampooned, and they might call toward falsehood and divert from the truth. In that is a deviation from the path of the human primordial nature built upon right guidance and calling toward the truth. Likewise, their saying what they do not do is a deviation from the path of the primordial nature.

The summary of the argument of the three verses is that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is not a poet, because the poets are followed by the erring due to their craft being built upon error and the opposite of right guidance. But those who follow him only follow him seeking right guidance and hitting upon the reality, and seeking the truth, because what he possesses of speech encompassing the call is built upon truth and right guidance rather than falsehood and error.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَذَكَرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً — ...excepting those who have faith, do righteous deeds, and remember Allah much (227) etc. An exception from the censured poets. The excepted ones are the poets of the believers; for faith and righteous deeds naturally deter a person from leaving the truth and following falsehood. Then, the frequent remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, makes a person mindful of Him, the Exalted, turning toward the truth that He approves and turning away from the falsehood that He does not love to be occupied with. Thus, what happened to those does not happen to these.

Through this explanation, the reason for qualifying the excepted ones with faith and the performance of righteous deeds, then conjoining His saying وَذَكَرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً (and remember Allah much) to that, becomes clear.

His saying: وَانْتَصَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا — ...and help themselves after they have been wronged (227). "Helping themselves" (الانتصار) means taking revenge. It has been said: What is meant by it is the response of the poets among the believers to the polytheists' poems in which they lampooned the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or attacked the religion and disparaged Islam and the Muslims. This is a good view supported by the context.

His saying: وَسَيَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَيَّ مُنْقَلَبٍ يَنْقَلِبُونَ — And the wrongdoers will soon know at what goal they will end up (227). The term المنقلب is a noun of place or a mim-infinitive. The meaning is: (And the wrongdoers will soon know)—and they are the polytheists according to what the context provides—to what place of return and departure they shall return and depart, which is the Fire; or what kind of overturning they shall be overturned.

In this is a threat to the polytheists and a return of the conclusion of the Surah to its opening; for at its beginning occurred His saying: فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنْبؤُا ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ — They have already denied [it], so soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding (6).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from al-Hajjal, from one whom he mentioned, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them), he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ (in a clear Arabic language). He said: يبين الألسن ولا تبينه الألسن — "It clarifies the [other] tongues, but the [other] tongues do not clarify it."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ نَزَّلْناهُ عَلى بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ etc. Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: لو نزلنا القرآن على العجم ما آمنت به العرب ـ وقد نزل على العرب فآمنت به العجم ـ فهذه فضيلة العجم — "Had We sent down the Quran to the non-Arabs, the Arabs would not have believed in it—but it was sent down to the Arabs and the non-Arabs believed in it—so this is a virtue of the non-Arabs."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from ‘Ali ibn ‘Isa al-Qammat, from his uncle, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was shown in his sleep the Umayyads—climbing upon his pulpit after him—and leading the people astray from the path backward—so he woke up distressed and sorrowful.

He said: Then Gabriel descended and said: "O Messenger of Allah, why do I see you distressed and sorrowful?" He said: "O Gabriel, I saw the Umayyads this night of mine—climbing my pulpit after me—leading the people astray from the path backward." He said: "By the One who sent you with the truth as a prophet, I was not aware of it." Then he ascended to heaven—and it was not long before he descended to him with verses from the Quran to comfort him with them. He said: أَفَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ مَتَّعْناهُمْ سِنِينَ ـ ثُمَّ جاءَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ ـ ما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يُمَتَّعُونَ (Tell Me, if We let them enjoy for some years, then there comes to them what they were promised, what they were given to enjoy will not avail them). And He sent down to him: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ ـ وَما أَدْراكَ ما لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ ـ لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ (Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment. And what will show you what is the Night of Ordainment? The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months). Allah made the Night of Ordainment for His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—better than a thousand months of the Umayyads' rule.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim extracted from Abu Jahdam, he said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was seen as if he were bewildered, so they asked him about that. He said: ولم ورأيت عدوي يلون أمر أمتي من بعدي — "And why [not], when I have seen my enemy taking charge of the affair of my nation after me?" Then there was revealed: أَفَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ مَتَّعْناهُمْ سِنِينَ ـ ثُمَّ جاءَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ ـ ما أَغْنى عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يُمَتَّعُونَ (Tell Me, if We let them enjoy for some years, then there comes to them what they were promised, what they were given to enjoy will not avail them), and his soul was comforted.

I say: His saying: ولم ورأيت etc., contains a deletion, and the estimation is: "And why should I not be so, when I have seen..." etc.

In the same source (ad-Durr al-Manthur), Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman and ad-Dala’il narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "When this verse was revealed: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations), the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله summoned the Quraysh, addressing them both generally and specifically. He said: 'O assembly of Quraysh! Save yourselves from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. O assembly of the children of Ka‘b ibn Lu’ay! Save yourselves from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. O assembly of the children of Qusay! Save yourselves from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. O assembly of the children of ‘Abd Manaf! Save yourselves from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. O children of ‘Abd al-Muttalib! Save yourselves from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. O Fatimah, daughter of Muhammad! Save yourself from the Fire, for I possess for you neither harm nor benefit. Behold, you have ties of kinship (رحماً) which I shall uphold with their due rights (سأبلها ببلالها).'"

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "When وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations) was revealed, he began calling them tribe by tribe."

In the same source, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, al-Bukhari, Ibn Mardawayh, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "When وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations) [and your sincere kinsmen among them] was revealed, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله went out until he climbed Mount al-Safa and called out: 'O people, a morning raid!' (يا صباحاه). They asked: 'Who is this calling out?' They said: 'It is Muhammad.' So they gathered around him. If a man was unable to go out himself, he would send a messenger to see what it was.

Abu Lahab and the Quraysh arrived, and he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Tell me, if I were to inform you that there are horsemen in the valley intending to raid you, would you believe me?' They replied: 'Yes, we have never experienced anything from you but truthfulness.' He said: 'Then I am a warner to you before a severe punishment.' Abu Lahab said: 'Perdition to you for the rest of the day! Is it for this that you have gathered us?' Then there was revealed: تَبَّتْ يَدا أَبِي لَهَبٍ وَتَبَّ (Perish the hands of Abu Lahab, and perish he!)."

In the same source, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Umamah, who said: "When وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah gathered the Banu Hashim and seated them at the door, and he gathered his wives and family and seated them in the house. Then he looked upon them and said: 'O Banu Hashim! Buy your souls from the Fire and strive for the liberation of your necks; ransom them yourselves from Allah, for I possess nothing for you against Allah.'

Then he turned to his household (أهل بيته) and said: 'O ‘A’ishah daughter of Abu Bakr, O Hafsah daughter of ‘Umar, O Umm Salamah, O Fatimah daughter of Muhammad, and O Umm al-Zubayr, aunt of the Messenger of Allah! Buy your souls from Allah and strive for the liberation of your necks, for I possess nothing for you against Allah, nor can I avail you anything...'" (the tradition continues).

I say: Other narrations carry the same meaning as these reports. In some of them, it is mentioned that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله specifically warned the Banu ‘Abd Manaf, which would include both the Banu Umayyah and the Banu Hashim.

However, the first three narrations do not align with the verse, as they generalize the warning to the Quraysh at large, whereas the verse explicitly mentions the "nearest relations" (العشيرة الأقربين), who are either the Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib or the Banu Hashim. The furthest narration from the verse is the second one, which states that he began calling them tribe by tribe.

Furthermore, the previously established meaning of the verse—which is the negation that kinship to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله can substitute for godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah—is alluded to in these narrations where he says: "I cannot avail you anything against Allah." This meaning is not suitable in its generality for those other than his close relatives صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

As for the fourth narration, the verse وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations) is a Meccan verse in a Meccan Surah, and no one has claimed that this verse was revealed in Medina. Where were ‘A’ishah, Hafsah, and Umm Salamah on the day of its revelation? The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not marry them until he was in Medina. Therefore, the reliable narrations are those indicating that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله specifically warned the Banu Hashim or the Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib on the day the verse was revealed. It is strange that al-Alusi, after citing these narrations, remarked: "If all are authentic, the way to reconcile them is to say that the warning occurred multiple times."

In al-Majma‘, from the Tafsir of al-Tha‘labi with his chain of transmission from Bara’ ibn ‘Azib, who said: "When this verse was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله gathered the Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib, who were forty men at that time—a man among them could eat a young sheep and drink a large vessel of milk (العس). He ordered ‘Ali to prepare a leg of mutton, which he seasoned. Then he said: 'Approach in the Name of Allah.' The people approached ten by ten and ate until they were satisfied. Then he called for a bowl of milk, took a sip from it, and said to them: 'Drink in the Name of Allah.' They drank until their thirst was quenched. Abu Lahab forestalled him and said: 'This is what the man has bewitched you with!' So the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله remained silent that day and did not speak.

The next day, he invited them again for similar food and drink. Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله warned them, saying: 'O Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib! I am indeed the warner to you from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; so submit (أسلموا) and obey me, and you shall be guided.'

Then he said: 'Who will brother me, assist me (يوازرني), and be my supporter (وليي), my legatee (وصيي) after me, and my successor (خليفتي) among my family, and pay my debt?' The people remained silent. He repeated it three times, and each time the people remained silent while ‘Ali said: 'I will.' On the third time, he said: 'You are the one.' The people stood up, saying to Abu Talib: 'Obey your son, for he has been made a commander over you!'"

Al-Tabarsi said: "This story is also narrated from Abu Rafi‘, stating that he gathered them in the mountain pass (الشعب). He prepared a leg of mutton for them, and they ate until they were full, and he gave them a large vessel of milk to drink, and they all drank until their thirst was quenched. Then he said: 'Indeed, Allah has commanded me to warn my kinsmen and my group. Allah has never sent a prophet except that He appointed for him from his family a brother, a vizier (وزيراً), an heir (وارثاً), a legatee (وصياً), and a successor (خليفة) among his family. Which of you, then, will stand and pledge allegiance to me on the basis that he is my brother, my heir, my vizier, and my legatee, and that he shall be to me as Aaron was to Moses?' ‘Ali said: 'I will.' He said: 'Come close to me.' Then he opened his mouth and applied some of his saliva into ‘Ali's mouth and spat between his shoulder blades and his chest. Abu Lahab said: 'What a wretched gift you have given your cousin—that he answered you and you filled his mouth and face with spittle!' The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله replied: 'I have filled him with wisdom and knowledge.'"

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated in ad-Durr al-Manthur the substance of the tradition of al-Bara’ from Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and al-Bayhaqi in ad-Dala’il through various paths from ‘Ali (may Allah be pleased with him). In it, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله spoke and said: "O Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib! By Allah, I do not know of anyone among the Arabs who has brought his people something better than what I have brought you. I have brought you the good of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah has commanded me to call you to Him. Which of you, then, will assist me in this matter of mine?" ‘Ali said: "I said, though I was the youngest of them: 'I will.' The people then stood up laughing."

In ‘Ilal al-Shara’i‘, with its chain of transmission from ‘Abdullah ibn al-Harith ibn Nawfal from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who said: "When وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations)—meaning your sincere kinsmen—was revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله summoned the Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib, who were forty men at that time, give or take one. He said: 'Which of you will be my brother, my heir, my vizier, my legatee, and my successor among you after me?' He presented this to them man by man, and all of them refused until he came to ‘Ali. I said: 'I will, O Messenger of Allah.'

He said: 'O Banu ‘Abd al-Muttalib! This is my heir, my vizier, and my successor among you after me.' The people stood up laughing at one another and saying to Abu Talib: 'He has commanded you to hear and obey this boy!'"

I say: It is possible to infer from his عليه‌السلام saying, "meaning your sincere kinsmen," that what was attributed to the recitation (قراءة) of the Ahl al-Bayt—وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ (And warn your nearest relations) [your sincere kinsmen among them]—and also attributed to the codex of Ubayy ibn Ka‘b, was in the nature of an interpretation (تفسير).

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِي السَّاجِدِينَ (and your moving about among those who prostrate), it is said: It means your transition among the prostrating monotheists (الموحدين) from prophet to prophet until He brought you forth as a prophet. This is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas in the report of ‘Ata’ and ‘Ikrimah, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who both said: "The loins of the prophets, prophet after prophet, until He brought him forth from the loin of his father through marriage, not through fornication, since the time of Adam."

I say: This has been narrated by other Shi‘i narrators, and it was narrated in ad-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym, and others from Ibn ‘Abbas and others.

In al-Majma‘, Jabir narrated from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Do not rise before me and do not descend before me, for I see you from behind me just as I see you from in front of me.' Then he recited this verse."

I say: He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله refers to placing the forehead on the ground and raising it during prostration (السجدة). This was also narrated in ad-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn ‘Abbas and others.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ahmad narrated from Abu Sa‘id, who said: "While we were traveling with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a poet appeared reciting poetry. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'For the belly of one of you to be filled with pus is better for him than to be filled with poetry.'"

I say: This is also narrated through Shi‘i paths from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام from him (the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, it is said: "They admonish people but do not take admonition themselves; they forbid evil but do not desist from it; they enjoin good but do not act upon it. They are those about whom Allah said: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي كُلِّ وادٍ يَهِيمُونَ (Do you not see that they roam in every valley?)—meaning they follow every path—وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ ما لا يَفْعَلُونَ (and that they say what they do not do?). They are those who usurped the right of the Progeny of Muhammad."

In the I‘tiqadat of al-Saduq, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ (As for the poets, [only] the perverse follow them). He said: "They are the storytellers (القصاص)."

I say: They are among the instances (المصاديق) of the verse, and the comprehensive meaning is what was mentioned previously in the commentary on the verse.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "Indeed, in some poetry there is wisdom (حكماً), and in some eloquence (البيان) there is sorcery (سحراً)."

I say: The first part was also narrated from him, from Buraydah and Ibn ‘Abbas from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and also from Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, with the wording: "Indeed, in some poetry there is wisdom (حكمة)." The praised form of poetry is that which contains support for the truth, and it is not included in the condemnation of the verse.

In al-Majma‘, from al-Zuhri, who said: "‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Ka‘b ibn Malik narrated to me that Ka‘b ibn Malik said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what do you say regarding the poets?' He replied: 'The believer strives with his sword and his tongue. By Him in Whose hand is my soul, it is as if you are pelting them with arrows.'"

Al-Tabarsi said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to Hassan ibn Thabit: 'Satirize them—or contend with them in satire—and the Holy Spirit (روح القدس) is with you.' This was narrated by al-Bukhari and Muslim in the two Sahihs."

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud in his Nasikh, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Hasan Salim al-Barrad, who said: "When the verse وَالشُّعَراءُ (As for the poets...) was revealed, ‘Abdullah ibn Rawahah, Ka‘b ibn Malik, and Hassan ibn Thabit came weeping. They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, Allah has revealed this verse, and He knows that we are poets; are we destroyed?' Then Allah revealed: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ (except those who have faith, do righteous deeds...). So the Messenger of Allah summoned them and recited it to them."

I say: This narration and those similar to it are what led some to claim that the last five verses of the Surah are Medinan. You have already seen the discussion regarding this in the commentary on the verses.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Abu ‘Ubaydah from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "Among the most difficult things Allah has obligated upon His creation is the frequent remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله كثيراً)." Then he said: "I do not mean [only] saying 'Glory be to Allah' (سبحان الله), 'Praise be to Allah' (الحمد لله), 'There is no god but Allah' (لا إله إلا الله), and 'Allah is Great' (الله أكبر)—though that is part of it—but rather the remembrance of Allah regarding what He has made lawful and prohibited; if it is an act of obedience, he performs it, and if it is an act of disobedience, he abandons it."

I say: In this there is support for what was mentioned previously in the commentary on the verse.
